meta.description: Orc origin story - played before prologue

orc_001.title: Born in Ash
text: You were born in the shadow of Reactor 7. That's what your mother told you, on the nights when the wind shifted and the air tasted like copper and ozone and the slow decay of the world that made you. Born in ash, she said. Born in the place where the old world died and something new — something harder, stranger, built for poison — crawled out of the wreckage.

The gene-plague zones stretch for miles beyond the city walls. Irradiated wastelands where the reactor cores that once powered civilization now bleed their invisible death into the soil and water and air. Nothing human survives there for long. But your people aren't human. Not anymore. Not for three generations, since the workers who maintained those reactors — the lowest caste, the expendable ones, the ones nobody bothered to evacuate when the containment failed — absorbed enough radiation to rewrite their children's DNA.

Grey-green skin. Bone density three times the human baseline. Musculature that grows dense and corded without effort. Toxin resistance that lets you breathe air that would kill a baseline human in hours. Tusks that grow from the lower jaw, small in childhood, prominent in adulthood. Eyes that see in spectrums the human eye cannot perceive. You are an orc. You are what happens when a species refuses to die, even when dying is the logical response to what's been done to it.

choices.text: Remember the clan. Remember where you came from.
Remember the naming ceremony. The day you became yourself.

orc_002.title: The Ironjaw Clan
text: The Ironjaw clan was two hundred strong at its peak. Two hundred orcs living in the ruins of Industrial Complex 7, a sprawling maze of collapsed factories and corroded storage tanks that your people had turned into something almost habitable. The walls were reinforced with scrap metal. The roofs were patched with industrial sheeting scavenged from the dead factories. Water came from a deep well dug through the contaminated topsoil to the aquifer below — still irradiated, but your bodies could handle it. Barely.

Your mother, Ghara, was the clan's bone-mender — the closest thing to a doctor your people had. She set fractures with splints made from reactor casings and treated radiation burns with poultices made from the mutant lichen that grew on the reactor walls. She was small for an orc — only six feet — with hands that could be impossibly gentle despite fingers thick enough to crush stone.

Your father — you never knew his name. Ghara didn't speak of him. Among the Ironjaw, a child belongs to the clan, not to parents. The concept of a nuclear family is a human weakness, Ghara said. Orcs are forged in community, tempered by the clan. Every adult is your parent. Every child is your sibling. The clan is the organism. You are the cell.

choices.text: The clan was hard but fair. You were raised with pride.
The clan was brutal. Survival was the only curriculum.

orc_003.title: Raised in Pride
text: The Ironjaw had traditions. Not the soft kind — no holidays, no celebrations of abundance — but the hard-forged rituals of a people who'd built meaning from nothing. The morning bell, rung from a rusted crane hook, that called the clan to the communal fire for the first meal. The evening songs — low, rumbling chants in a language that was part human speech, part something new, something born in the reactors — that told the history of your people in a cadence that vibrated through your chest.

Elder Kurg taught the young ones. He was ancient by orc standards — sixty, maybe sixty-five, his skin cracked and weathered like old concrete, his tusks yellowed and chipped from decades of hard use. He taught you to fight, to forage, to read the radiation winds. But more than that, he taught you what it meant to be an orc.

'Humans made us,' he said, his voice a deep rasp like metal dragging on stone. 'They put our ancestors in those reactors because orc lives — human lives, then — were cheap. And when the containment failed and the plague changed us, they called us monsters. But we are not what they made us. We are what we made ourselves. Remember that. Every day. Every breath. We chose to survive. We chose to be more than what they intended.'
You believed him. You still do.

choices.text: The strength rituals began when you were ten.

orc_004.title: The Hard Way
text: The Ironjaw didn't coddle. Couldn't afford to. In the gene-plague zones, weakness meant death — not as a metaphor but as a biological fact. The radiation storms could strip exposed skin in minutes. The mutant fauna — things that had once been dogs and rats and birds, warped by the same forces that had made your people — hunted in packs and feared nothing. The water had to be filtered through three stages of processing before it wouldn't make you vomit blood, and even then it tasted like iron.

Children who couldn't keep up were carried until they could walk, and then they walked or they didn't. That sounds cruel by human standards. By orc standards, it was mercy — the alternative was letting the weak drag the strong into the grave. Your people had learned, through generations of dying, that compassion without pragmatism is just a slower form of suicide.

You were strong. Even by orc standards, you were strong. By the time you were eight, you could carry your own body weight across the scrap yards without stopping. By ten, you could fight — not the formalized sparring of the training grounds but the raw, desperate violence of a child defending their meal from something with more teeth and less conscience.
The scars on your arms are a map of your education.

choices.text: The strength rituals began when you were ten.

orc_005.title: The Strength Ritual
text: Every orc child undergoes the Trial of Iron. It is the first of three rituals that mark the passage from child to warrior, and it is the one that breaks most who attempt it. Elder Kurg led you and six other ten-year-olds to the base of the crane tower — a skeletal structure of rusted steel that rises two hundred feet from the floor of the old factory, swaying in the wind like a dead tree.

The trial is simple: climb. From the base to the top. No rope. No harness. No assistance. The metal is corroded, the handholds treacherous, the wind strong enough at the higher levels to pull a grown orc from the girders. Below, nothing but concrete and scrap metal and a fall that would end everything.
Kurg didn't give a speech. He just pointed upward and said: 'The clan is watching.'

The clan was always watching. That was the point. Every orc in the Ironjaw compound had gathered below the crane tower, their amber eyes tracking the climbers, their voices silent. No cheers. No encouragement. In orcish culture, the ritual speaks for itself. You either reach the top or you don't. Both outcomes are accepted without judgment.

choices.text: You climbed without hesitation. The height meant nothing to you.
You were terrified. But you climbed anyway.

orc_006.title: The Fearless Climb
text: Your hands found the metal and your body knew what to do. Grip, pull, climb. The rust bit into your palms and the wind tried to tear you loose and you didn't care. The height wasn't a threat — it was a perspective. With every meter you gained, the world below shrank and the sky grew larger, and you felt something expanding in your chest that you later understood was the first taste of your own strength.

You reached the top before any of the others. At the peak of the crane tower, two hundred feet above the compound, you could see the gene-plague zones stretching to the horizon — miles of ruined industry and poisoned earth, and beyond it, like a mirage made of steel and light, the distant silhouette of the city. Your future. Your destiny. Though you didn't know it yet.

The wind hit you full force at the top, and you braced against it and opened your mouth and let out a sound that came from somewhere deeper than your lungs — a roar that the clan below heard and recognized. Not a word. A declaration. An announcement to the world that you existed and you were strong and you would not be moved.
Three of the seven made it to the top that day. You were the first.

choices.text: The second trial came a year later. The Trial of Blood.

orc_007.title: The Terrified Climb
text: Your hands shook on the first rung. The metal was cold and rough and your palms were sweating and the ground was right there, right below you, solid and safe and everything that the empty air above was not. You looked up at the tower — that impossible column of rust and shadow stretching into the grey sky — and your body said no. Every nerve, every instinct, every animal part of your brain screamed at you to let go and step back and accept that some things were too high to reach.

You climbed anyway. Not because you weren't afraid. Because you were afraid and you did it anyway, and that — Kurg taught you later — is the true meaning of the Trial of Iron. The fearless ones prove their strength. The terrified ones prove their will. And will, in the long run, outlasts strength.

You fell twice. The first time, at thirty feet, your hand slipped and you dropped ten feet before catching a cross-beam that dislocated your shoulder. You hung there, one-armed, the pain blinding, the ground below waiting. Then you set your jaw, pulled yourself up with your good arm, and kept climbing. The second fall was at one hundred feet. You caught yourself after a five-foot drop and didn't stop again.
At the top, shaking and bloodied and burning with a pain that felt like transformation, you raised your face to the sky and wept. Below, the clan watched in silence.

choices.text: The second trial came a year later. The Trial of Blood.

orc_008.title: The Trial of Blood
text: The Trial of Blood is not what humans think it is. They hear the name and imagine savagery — orcs beating each other bloody in some ritualized display of dominance. The truth is quieter and, in its way, more demanding.

The trial requires you to bleed for someone else. Not in combat. In service. For one full cycle of the moon, you are assigned to care for an elder — one of the clan's aged, those whose bodies have begun to fail, whose radiation-scarred lungs struggle with each breath. You feed them. You clean them. You carry them when they can't walk and sit with them through the nights when the pain makes sleep impossible.

Your elder was Tohma. She was seventy-three, ancient beyond measure for an orc, her body a landscape of tumors and scar tissue. Her eyes had been blinded by a radiation storm twenty years before, and her hands trembled with a palsy that made feeding herself impossible. She spoke rarely, and when she did, her voice was like wind through broken pipes.

For thirty days, Tohma was your world. You learned patience. You learned gentleness — a word that sits oddly in an orc's mouth but fits perfectly in an orc's hands. You learned that strength without tenderness is just another form of violence, and violence without purpose is just noise.

choices.text: Tohma told you stories during those thirty days. Stories that shaped you.
Tohma died on the twenty-eighth day. You carried her body to the pyre.

orc_009.title: Tohma's Stories
text: Tohma remembered the before. Not many did — most of the orcs alive today were born after the gene-plague, born into bodies already changed. But Tohma remembered being human. She remembered her name — her human name, which she never told you, because orcs don't look backward. She remembered working in Reactor 7's cooling system, a job that paid minimum wage and required no qualifications beyond a willingness to accept the risk.

'They told us the containment was safe,' she said, her blind eyes turned toward the sound of your breathing. 'They said the readings were within acceptable parameters. Acceptable. That's a human word, isn't it? It means: the risk is real, but it's happening to someone else, so it's acceptable.'

She told you about the day the containment failed. The alarms. The locked doors. The workers trapped inside the reactor building while the radiation levels climbed past survivable into something the monitoring equipment wasn't calibrated to measure. How they pounded on the doors and screamed for help and the doors stayed locked because the protocol prioritized containment over evacuation.

'We didn't die,' Tohma said. 'We changed. And they were more afraid of what we became than they ever were of what they did to us.' She reached out with her trembling hand and touched your face. 'You are the proof, child. The proof that they failed. They tried to kill us and instead they made us stronger. Remember that. Always remember.'

choices.text: The naming ceremony came next. The day you chose who you would be.

orc_010.title: The Naming Ceremony
text: Among the Ironjaw, you are born without a name. For the first twelve years of your life, you are called 'child' — no differentiation, no individual identity. You are the clan's child, belonging to everyone and no one, your identity submerged in the collective until the day you earn the right to separate from it.

The naming ceremony is the third trial, the culmination of the passage from child to warrior. It takes place at the communal fire, with the entire clan gathered in a circle — two hundred pairs of amber eyes reflecting the flames, two hundred bodies forming a wall of muscle and bone and shared history. The silence is absolute. Even the wind seems to hold its breath.
Elder Kurg stands at the fire's edge. In his hands, a branding iron heated white-hot, its tip shaped into the Ironjaw clan symbol: a stylized jaw with interlocking teeth.
'Who comes to the fire?' Kurg asks. His voice carries across the compound like a physical force.
'A child,' the clan responds in unison.
'What does the child seek?'
'A name.'

choices.text: You step forward. You are ready.

orc_011.title: Tohma's Pyre
text: She died in her sleep. That was the mercy — the only one the gene-plague zones offered, and it offered it rarely. You woke to silence where her breathing should have been, and you knew before you touched her hand, before you felt the coolness of skin that no longer carried life. You sat with her for an hour. Not because of ritual or requirement, but because leaving felt like betrayal.

The pyre was built from scrap wood and reactor casings, stacked on the high ground where the wind was strongest. Orcs don't bury their dead — the contaminated soil would corrupt the remains, and orcish tradition holds that the dead should return to the sky, not the earth. The fire burns hot enough to reduce bone to powder, and the wind carries the ash across the wasteland like a final migration.

You lit the pyre yourself. That was your right as her caretaker, the one who'd bled for her during the Trial of Blood. The flames caught the wood and climbed the casings and consumed what was left of Tohma — the last person alive who remembered being human, who remembered the moment the world broke and something new crawled from the wreckage.
The clan watched in silence. No words. Words are for the living.

choices.text: After Tohma, the naming ceremony. The day you chose your name.

orc_012.title: The Branding
text: You step into the firelight. The heat hits you like a wall — not the ambient warmth of the compound's heating fires but the focused intensity of a pyre built for purpose. The branding iron glows white in Kurg's hands, and the clan symbol at its tip seems to pulse with a life of its own.
'Speak your name,' Kurg says.

This is the moment. In orcish tradition, your name is not given to you — it is claimed. You choose it, speak it before the clan, and bind it to yourself with fire. The name must reflect who you are, who you intend to be. It carries weight. It carries obligation. Among the Ironjaw, your name is your contract with the future.

You speak. The name comes from somewhere deep — from the climb, from the blood, from the stories Tohma told you, from the ash and iron and radiation that made your people. It fills the silence like a stone dropped into still water, and the clan hears it, and the clan accepts it.

Kurg presses the brand to your left shoulder. The pain is extraordinary — a white-hot lance that drives through skin and muscle and seems to touch bone. You don't scream. You don't flinch. The brand sizzles against your flesh and the smell of your own burning fills your nostrils, and you stand in the fire's light and bear it because this is the price of a name, and you will pay it in full.

choices.text: The years after the naming were good. The clan thrived.
The naming was the last good day. Everything changed soon after.

orc_013.title: The Good Years
text: For a time — a brief, shining time that burns brighter in memory than it probably deserved — the Ironjaw thrived. The hunters found new migration patterns in the mutant herds that roamed the wasteland. The scavengers discovered an untouched cache of pre-plague machinery in a collapsed warehouse, and the clan's smiths — orcs who'd taught themselves metallurgy from necessity — forged tools and weapons from the salvage. The well water tested cleaner than it had in years.

You grew into your name. You trained with the warriors, learning the fighting style unique to orcs: close-quarters, overwhelming force, the use of mass and momentum and the kind of raw physical power that no human martial art accounts for. You could bench-press a reactor casing by fourteen. You could run for hours without stopping. Your body was an engine built for survival in conditions that would kill anything softer.

And there was Bren. Bren of the broad shoulders and the quiet laugh and the hands that could shape metal into beauty. Bren who sat with you on the crane tower on clear nights and pointed out the city's distant lights and wondered aloud what it would be like to walk streets that weren't made of rubble. Bren who looked at you like you were the only fixed point in a world that wouldn't stop spinning.

choices.text: Then the corporations came. They always come.

orc_014.title: The Change
text: The radiation storms increased that year. Not gradually — suddenly, as if some underground threshold had been crossed. Storms that used to come once a season now hit every few weeks, each one stronger than the last. The sky would turn green, the air would taste like burning copper, and the clan would retreat to the deepest shelters and wait, listening to the howl of irradiated wind tearing at the structures above.

The elders died first. Their bodies, already compromised by decades of exposure, couldn't handle the intensified radiation. Three elders in one season. Then five. Then Kurg himself, the ancient one, the teacher, who sat down after a particularly brutal storm and simply didn't stand up again. His last words were in the old tongue — the pre-plague language of the workers who'd become the first orcs — and nobody alive could translate them.

The hunters reported changes in the wasteland too. The mutant herds were migrating away from the reactor zones, driven by the same force that was killing the elders. The water table was rising, which should have been good news except that the rising water was bringing deeper contamination to the surface. The well readings spiked. Even orc physiology had limits.
The clan was dying. Slowly, but dying. And you — newly named, newly strong, newly terrified in a way the Trial of Iron had never prepared you for — watched it happen.

choices.text: The clan's elders debated: stay and adapt, or seek the city. You had an opinion.

orc_015.title: The Corporation
text: They arrived in armored transports — three of them, painted in the blue and silver of Helion Industries, cutting across the wasteland like predators moving into new hunting grounds. The transports parked outside the compound in a formation that was technically non-threatening and functionally a siege line. Twenty armed security personnel deployed around them, not pointing their weapons at anyone but not holstering them either.

A man in a clean suit stepped out of the lead transport. He was the cleanest thing you'd ever seen — pressed fabric, polished shoes, skin that had never been touched by radiation or acid rain. He carried a tablet and a smile, and both were weapons.

'Good morning,' he said, as if two hundred orcs staring at him from the compound walls was a normal Tuesday. 'My name is Director Hale, and I'm here on behalf of Helion Industries to discuss a mutually beneficial arrangement regarding the mineral resources beneath your settlement.'
Mutually beneficial. Even then, before you'd learned the human language of exploitation, the words felt wrong. Like honey poured over something rotten.

choices.text: The clan chief negotiated. There were terms. There were lies.
You wanted to fight. Drive them off. But the chief chose diplomacy.

orc_016.title: The Debate
text: The council fire burned high as the surviving elders and warriors debated the clan's future. Orcish councils are not democratic in the human sense — there is no vote, no majority rule. Instead, each voice speaks until it has been heard, and the chief makes the final decision, bearing the weight of every argument on their shoulders.

Garok, the war-leader, spoke for staying. 'This is our land,' he said, his voice rolling like thunder across the compound. 'Our ancestors' ashes are in this soil. We were forged here. We die here if we must, but we do not run.'

Meren, the scout-leader, spoke for leaving. 'The land is killing us,' she said, quieter but no less fierce. 'There is no honor in dying for dirt that poisons our children. The city has work. The city has shelter. We go, we survive, we build strength. And when we are strong enough, we come back.'
The chief, old Durga with her missing eye and her iron hand, listened to both. Then she looked at you — newly named, youngest warrior in the circle — and said: 'And what does the young one say?'

choices.text: "We stay. We adapt. This is home."
"We go to the city. Survival first. Pride second."
"We send scouts first. Learn what the city offers before we decide."

orc_017.title: The Terms
text: Director Hale's terms were presented on a tablet with graphs and projections and numbers that danced across the screen in colors that hurt your eyes. Stripped of the corporate language, they were simple: Helion wanted the mineral rights beneath the Ironjaw compound. In exchange, the clan would receive 'relocation assistance' — transport to the city's Lower District, temporary housing, and 'employment opportunities' in Helion's industrial operations.

Employment opportunities. Another human euphemism. What Hale meant, and what the chief understood but pretended not to, was labor contracts. Indentured work in Helion's mines and factories, the same kind of work that had put your ancestors in the reactors three generations ago. The wheel turning back to its starting position.

Chief Durga negotiated. She was shrewd — decades of leading an orc clan in a wasteland had made her harder than the irradiated bedrock beneath the compound. She demanded better housing, higher wages, healthcare access, education for the young. Hale agreed to everything. His smile never changed.

You watched from the back of the council and felt the hair on your arms rise — the instinctive orc response to deception. Something in Hale's scent, in the way his heartbeat remained perfectly steady while he made promises he had no intention of keeping. Your body knew he was lying. But the chief had made her decision.

choices.text: The clan relocated to the city. The promises were broken within weeks.

orc_018.title: The Urge to Fight
text: Every muscle in your body wanted to charge. The orc instinct — bred into you by three generations of survival in the deadliest environment on the planet — screamed at you to pick up the nearest piece of rebar and drive these clean, soft-skinned invaders back to their transports and beyond. You were an orc. You were built for violence the way the reactor was built for fission. This was your land and these people were trespassers.

But Durga chose diplomacy. And Durga was chief, and the chief's word was the clan's law, and three hundred years of orcish tradition held you in place more surely than any chain. You stood at the back of the crowd with your fists clenched and your tusks bared and your heart pounding with a rage that had nowhere to go.

Bren found you after the meeting, sitting on a reactor casing in the dark, your knuckles bleeding from punching a wall. 'I know,' Bren said, sitting beside you. 'I feel it too. But Durga sees something we don't. She always does.'
'She sees twenty armed soldiers,' you said. 'She sees what happens to two hundred orcs who fight a corporation with resources to burn.' Bren was quiet. You were both quiet for a long time.

choices.text: The clan relocated. You went with them, burning with anger.

orc_019.title: Speaking for Home
text: 'We stay,' you said, your voice raw but steady. 'This is where we were forged. Tohma's ashes are in this wind. Kurg's brand is on every warrior's shoulder. If we leave, we leave ourselves behind.' The council fire crackled. Two hundred eyes watched you. 'We are orcs. We were made in poison and fire. What kills others makes us strong. We adapt. We endure. We stay.'

Garok nodded, his massive arms crossed, approval radiating from him like heat. Meren shook her head slowly, not in anger but in the resigned recognition of someone who's heard the argument before and knows how it ends.
Durga listened. Then she closed her eye — the remaining one, amber and bright — and sat in silence for a long time. When she opened it, her decision was made.

'We stay,' she said. 'We dig deeper. We find cleaner water. We build stronger shelters. And if the storms take us, they take orcs who died on their own ground, not refugees begging at a city's gates.'

choices.text: You stayed. For a while. Until the storms made staying impossible.

orc_020.title: Speaking for Survival
text: 'We go,' you said, and the word felt like a betrayal even as it left your mouth. 'I love this ground as much as any orc who walks it. But the ground is poisoned, and our children are dying, and pride is a poor meal for hungry bellies.' You met Garok's eyes — saw the flash of anger there, the warrior's contempt for retreat. You didn't flinch. 'Kurg taught me that orcs survive. That's what we do. That's what we are. We didn't survive the reactors by standing still. We survived by changing. The city is just another reactor. We'll change again.'

The silence after you spoke lasted long enough for the fire to crack and send sparks spiraling upward. Then Meren spoke: 'The young one has wisdom.' And Durga, the chief, the old warrior with the missing eye, looked at you and nodded once.
'We go,' Durga said. 'But we go as Ironjaw. We go together. And we go remembering who we are and what was done to us. The city will try to make us forget. We will not forget.'

choices.text: The migration to the city was harder than anyone expected.

orc_021.title: The Scout's Approach
text: 'Send me,' you said. 'Send me and three others. We go to the city. We learn its rhythms, its dangers, its opportunities. We come back with knowledge, and then the clan decides with clear eyes instead of blind hope or blind fear.'

Durga regarded you for a long time. Then, slowly, the ghost of a smile. 'Kurg would have liked you,' she said. 'He always preferred thinking to hitting. Said it was the harder discipline.' She turned to the council. 'The young one speaks sense. We send scouts. Four. Armed but not hostile. They go, they learn, they return. Then we decide.'

You left the next morning with three companions: Meren, the scout-leader; Volk, a quiet warrior with a talent for observation; and Bren, because Bren refused to let you go without them and nobody argued with Bren when they'd made up their mind.

The wasteland between the compound and the city was forty miles of irradiated nothing. You covered it in three days, sleeping in ruins, eating dried meat, watching the city grow on the horizon from a distant glimmer to a concrete and steel reality that dwarfed anything you'd imagined.

choices.text: The city was nothing like you expected.

orc_022.title: Relocation
text: Helion's transports came at dawn. Twelve vehicles, each large enough to carry twenty orcs and their meager possessions. The Ironjaw loaded what they could: tools, weapons, the communal cooking implements, the branding iron wrapped in leather and ash-cloth. Everything else — the shelters they'd built, the gardens they'd coaxed from toxic soil, the pyres where their dead had been returned to the sky — was left behind. Within hours of the clan's departure, Helion's mining equipment would begin tearing it apart.

The ride to the city took four hours. Four hours in a metal box with no windows, the drone of the engine a constant vibration that you felt in your teeth. The younger children were frightened. The elders were silent. Bren sat beside you, their hand on your knee, and you stared at the metal wall in front of you and counted breaths.

The city appeared all at once when they opened the transport doors. A wall of noise and light and smell that hit your orc senses like a physical assault. After a lifetime in the wasteland's stark silence, the sensory overload was staggering. You literally staggered — a step backward, your hand on the transport's wall, your lungs struggling with air that tasted like a hundred chemicals you'd never encountered.
Director Hale was there, smiling. 'Welcome to your new home,' he said. 'Housing is this way.'

choices.text: The 'housing' was a warehouse. The 'employment' was a labor camp.

orc_023.title: The Breaking Storm
text: The final storm came on a night that started clear. A deception — the air went still, the sky showed stars for the first time in months, and the clan gathered outside to look upward at points of light most of them had never seen. Then the horizon turned green. Not the faint green of a minor storm, but a vivid, pulsing emerald that painted the entire western sky with sick light.

The storm hit like a wall. Wind strong enough to tear sheet metal from the shelters. Rain that burned on contact — not the mild acid of the usual precipitation but something caustic, something that raised blisters on orc skin that had weathered decades of contamination. The shelters buckled. The deep shelters flooded with contaminated water that rose ankle-deep, waist-deep, chest-deep.

Seven orcs died that night. Seven. Including two children who drowned in the flooding shelters because the drainage system collapsed under the unprecedented volume. You pulled a third child from the water with hands that were burned raw by the storm's chemical rain, and you held her while she screamed, and you looked at Durga's face in the emergency lighting and saw the decision form before she spoke it.
'We go,' Durga said. Her voice was the quietest you'd ever heard it. 'We go now.'

choices.text: The migration was desperate, unplanned, brutal.

orc_024.title: The Long Walk
text: Two hundred orcs walking across forty miles of irradiated wasteland. No transports. No supplies beyond what could be carried. The sick on stretchers borne by the strong. The children clustered in the center of the column, surrounded by warriors. The elders at whatever pace they could manage, pride keeping them upright long past the point where their bodies wanted to fold.

The journey took five days. Five days of walking through terrain that fought every step — crumbling roads, chemical streams that had to be forded, ruins inhabited by things that watched from the shadows with eyes that reflected no light. At night, the clan made camp in whatever shelter the landscape offered, and the warriors formed a perimeter, and the fires were lit, and the old songs were sung — quiet now, almost whispers, as if the clan was afraid the wasteland would hear and try to swallow the sound.

You lost eight more on the walk. Four elders who couldn't keep pace and refused to be carried, who sat down by the roadside and told the clan to go on, who died looking at the sky with expressions of relief. Two warriors taken by a pack of mutant stalkers in a night attack. A mother and child, taken by fever.
When the city appeared on the horizon, you didn't feel relief. You felt the weight of everyone you'd left behind.

choices.text: The city's gates were not welcoming.

orc_025.title: The Scout's View
text: Nothing in the wasteland prepared you for the city. The scale alone was staggering — buildings stacked so high they disappeared into a permanent canopy of smog and weather, connected by bridges and catwalks that spanned chasms of open air. The noise was a physical presence: engines, voices, machinery, the constant hum of a million lives compressed into a space built for half that number.

But it was the people that shocked you. Humans — more humans than you'd ever imagined existed — moving through the streets in dense, purposeful streams. And among them, other races: synths with their too-smooth faces, cyborgs with chrome limbs, elves who moved through the crowds like ghosts. And orcs. Other orcs. Not Ironjaw — different clans, different brands — but recognizable. Your people, scattered through the city like seeds in hostile soil.

These city orcs looked different from you. Worn thinner. The wildness filed down. They walked with their shoulders hunched and their heads lowered, taking up less space than their bodies needed, as if trying to apologize for their own existence. They worked the heaviest jobs — construction, demolition, waste processing — and were paid the least for it. You watched an orc twice your age carrying a steel beam on his shoulders while a human supervisor screamed at him to move faster, and something in your chest tightened like a fist.
Bren stood beside you, watching the same scene. 'Is this what survival looks like?' Bren asked. Neither of you answered.

choices.text: You returned to the clan with your report. The truth was hard to deliver.

orc_026.title: The Warehouse
text: The 'housing' was a converted industrial warehouse in Sector 9. Concrete floor. Metal walls. A single row of chemical toilets along one side. No beds — just floor space, marked out in chalk rectangles, each one assigned to a family unit or individual. The rectangles were sized for humans. An orc lying down would overlap into the adjacent space. Director Hale's smile never wavered as he walked the Ironjaw through their new 'home.'

The 'employment opportunities' materialized three days later. Labor contracts — the real kind, the kind with fine print and penalty clauses and termination conditions that required a legal degree to parse. The jobs were in Helion's processing plants: heavy industrial work, twelve-hour shifts, the kind of labor that exploited orc strength while paying orc wages, which were exactly sixty percent of human wages for the same work because the Fair Employment Act of 2138 contained a rider that classified 'gene-plague descendants' as a separate labor category.
Sixty percent. They'd built discrimination into the mathematics.

Ghara looked at the contract with eyes that had seen the containment failure and the first mutations and three generations of struggle, and she said: 'They did this to our ancestors. They put our people in the reactors because it was cheaper than machines. Now they put us in the factories for the same reason. The only thing that's changed is the uniform.'

choices.text: You worked in the plants. You had no choice.
You refused the contract. You'd find your own way.

orc_027.title: The Gates
text: The Lower District gate was a bottleneck of steel and bureaucracy. Security checkpoints, biometric scanners, registration terminals. A line of people — humans, mostly, with the occasional synth or elf — queued in orderly formation, showing IDs and permits and the patient compliance of subjects who'd internalized the system's demands.

Two hundred orcs arriving at once was not something the gate was designed to handle. The security personnel — human, augmented, armed with crowd-control weapons — reacted the way armed people always react to large groups of orcs: with barely concealed panic disguised as protocol.

'HALT. FORM A LINE. PRODUCE IDENTIFICATION.' The amplified voice crackled from speakers mounted on the wall. Identification. Your people had never needed identification. In the wasteland, your face was your ID and your clan brand was your passport.

Durga walked to the front of the column. A woman half the height of the gate guards, one-eyed, iron-handed, sixty years of wasteland survival in the set of her jaw. She looked up at the armed humans and said: 'We are the Ironjaw. We have walked forty miles through poisoned land. Our children are hungry and our elders are dying. You will let us in, or you will explain to your superiors why two hundred orcs are camped outside your gate, attracting the kind of attention your superiors prefer to avoid.'

choices.text: They let you in. Eventually. After hours of waiting.

orc_028.title: The Report
text: You stood before the council fire and told the truth. Not the optimistic truth or the palatable truth, but the full, unvarnished truth: the city was vast, rich in resources, and hostile to orcs in ways both obvious and insidious. The work was available but degrading. The housing was inadequate. The humans viewed orcs as labor, not as people. The other orc clans in the city had survived, but survival had cost them something essential — the wildness, the pride, the identity that made them Ironjaw.

Meren backed your assessment. Volk said nothing, but his silence spoke volumes. Bren added a detail you'd missed: 'They look at us the way we look at the mutant herds. Not with hatred. With the cold calculation of predators assessing prey.'
Durga listened to it all. Then she made her decision, as chiefs do, weighing the bad against the worse.
'We go,' she said. 'Not because the city is good. Because the wasteland is dying. And I will not lead my people into a grave. A cage is at least a place where you can plan an escape.'

choices.text: The clan migrated. All of you, together.

orc_029.title: The Processing Plant
text: The Helion Processing Plant in Sector 9 was a cathedral of industry. Massive, roaring, perpetually hot, filled with the deafening cacophony of machines that processed raw ore into refined materials. The work was simple and devastating: load the crushers, monitor the conveyors, clear the blockages that occurred when the machinery jammed. The blockages were the dangerous part — reaching into a temporarily halted crusher to clear compacted ore, knowing that a sensor error or a premature restart would turn you into part of the product.

Orc workers were placed on the heaviest lines. Human workers got the control rooms, the monitoring stations, the jobs that required brains instead of backs. The segregation wasn't official — the employee handbook used the word 'assignment' and based it on 'physical capability assessments' — but the result was the same. Orcs in the heat. Humans in the cool. Orcs carrying. Humans watching.

You worked alongside an orc from the Ashclaw clan named Torr. He'd been in the city for eight years. He'd watched three clan members die in industrial accidents. 'They don't fix the machines because it's cheaper to replace us,' he said. Not with anger. With the flat resignation of someone stating a physical law. Gravity pulls down. Water flows downhill. Corporations consume orc lives.

choices.text: An accident on the line forced a reckoning.
You endured. For the clan. For Bren. For the life you were trying to build.

orc_030.title: Refusing the Contract
text: Ghara called you stubborn. Durga called you foolish. Bren called you brave. They were all correct.

You left the warehouse with nothing but your clothes and the clan brand on your shoulder, and you walked into the Lower District's maze of streets and alleys and vertical slums and tried to build something on your own terms. An orc alone in the city is a conspicuous thing — your size, your skin, your tusks draw attention that ranges from curiosity to hostility, and navigating between the two requires a diplomacy you hadn't learned in the wasteland.

Work came hard. The legitimate jobs required identification you didn't have and references you couldn't provide. The underground economy was more flexible but more dangerous — you hauled cargo for a smuggling operation, stood guard for a gambling den, did demolition work for a construction crew that paid cash and didn't file safety reports. The money was enough for food but not enough for shelter, and for the first two months in the city you slept in a drainage tunnel that reminded you, with cruel irony, of the reactor corridors where your people were first transformed.

But you were free. Free from Helion's contracts, free from the warehouse's chalk rectangles, free from the system that wanted to reduce you to a line item in a labor report. The freedom tasted like hunger and rain and the specific loneliness of being an orc in a human city.

choices.text: You found the orc underground. Other clans, other exiles.
You kept to yourself. An orc without a clan is something new.

orc_031.title: Inside the Walls
text: They processed you like cargo. Biometric scans, registration numbers, temporary resident permits stamped with the gene-plague descendant classification that marked you in every database as something other than fully human. The processing took twelve hours. Twelve hours of standing in lines, answering questions from bureaucrats who couldn't pronounce your names, being photographed and fingerprinted and cataloged by a system that had never anticipated having to accommodate two hundred orcs at once.

The children fell asleep on the processing center floor. The elders sat in rigid silence, their pride a shell around the exhaustion inside. Durga stood throughout, her iron hand resting on the counter while yet another clerk entered yet another form, her one eye fixed on a point somewhere beyond the walls, beyond the city, beyond this moment.

They assigned the Ironjaw to temporary housing in Sector 9. A warehouse. Of course a warehouse. The city's solution to every inconvenient population: store them somewhere out of sight and hope the problem resolves itself.

You walked through streets lit by neon signs advertising things you'd never heard of, your boots crunching on pavement instead of rubble, and felt the walls of the buildings pressing in like the sides of a cage. The sky was invisible behind the city's canopy. For the first time in your life, you couldn't see the stars.

choices.text: The clan settled in. Adapted. Or tried to.

orc_032.title: The Accident
text: The crusher jammed at 1400 on a shift that had already been running three hours over standard because the quota hadn't been met. The supervisor — a human, always a human — ordered the blockage cleared manually. Protocol required the machine to be powered down and locked out before anyone entered the crushing chamber. The supervisor overrode the lockout because powering down the machine would cost thirty minutes of production.

Torr went in. He was the biggest, the most experienced, the one who'd cleared a hundred blockages before. He reached into the chamber with arms that could bend rebar and braced against the compacted ore, and for a moment it seemed like the blockage would clear.

The crusher restarted. A sensor glitch, the subsequent investigation concluded. An automated restart triggered by the blockage clearing, which the system interpreted as normal operation. Torr lost his left arm below the elbow. The sound he made — not a scream, orcs don't scream, but a deep, guttural bellow of shock and agony — echoed through the plant and stopped every worker on the floor.

The medical response took fourteen minutes. Fourteen minutes while Torr bled from a wound that would have killed a human in three. Orc physiology saved his life. Orc physiology, not Helion's safety protocols, not the medical team that arrived too late with equipment designed for human bodies.

choices.text: You confronted the supervisor. Physically.
You filed a formal complaint. Tried to use the system against itself.
You quit. Walked out. Enough was enough.

orc_033.title: Endurance
text: You endured because that's what orcs do. It's in the blood, in the bone, in the genetic legacy of ancestors who survived reactor meltdowns and gene-plagues and the systematic abandonment of an entire caste of human beings. Endurance isn't a virtue for orcs — it's a biological imperative, coded into every cell, expressed in every breath.

You worked twelve-hour shifts and came home to the warehouse and ate the inadequate rations Helion provided and slept on a chalk rectangle and woke up and did it again. And again. And again. The routine was grinding but not unfamiliar — the wasteland had its own rhythms, its own relentless cycles. At least here, the thing trying to kill you was predictable.

Bren worked the same plant, different line. You saw each other at shift change — brief moments in the corridor, a touch of hands, a few words exchanged over the roar of machinery. 'How are you?' 'Alive.' 'Good enough.' This was your love language now. The minimalism of exhaustion.

Ghara set up a medical station in the warehouse, treating the injuries that the plant's medical team ignored or undertreated. Burns, fractures, the long-term damage of working around industrial chemicals that human safety standards didn't account for because the standards weren't designed for orc physiology.

choices.text: Something happened that broke the endurance. A breaking point.

orc_034.title: The Orc Underground
text: They gathered in the sub-levels beneath Sector 6, in a network of abandoned maintenance tunnels that the city had forgotten and the orcs had claimed. The Orc Underground — they called it the Warren — was not a single organization but a loose confederation of exiles, outcasts, and refugees from a dozen different clans. Ashclaw, Stonefist, Blacktusk, Redmaw. Names that carried the weight of wasteland histories, burned into shoulders and chests and foreheads, marking each orc as a member of a story that stretched back to the reactor failures.

The Warren's leader — if 'leader' was the right word for someone who organized through consensus and force of personality rather than formal authority — was a massive orc named Grund. He'd been in the city for fifteen years, longer than any other orc in the Warren, and he'd built the underground network from nothing. Shelters, supply caches, safe routes through the city's sub-levels, connections with sympathetic humans and synths who could provide medical care, legal assistance, and the occasional warning when Sec-Corp raids were planned.
Grund looked at your Ironjaw brand and nodded. 'Another clan pushed off its land,' he said. Not a question. 'Same story. Different chapter. Welcome to the Warren. We're all exiles here.'

choices.text: The Warren became your new clan. Different, but not lesser.

orc_035.title: The Clanless Orc
text: An orc without a clan is a contradiction in terms. Orcish identity is communal — your name comes from the clan fire, your brand comes from the clan iron, your history is the clan's history. To be clanless is to be unnamed in a way that goes deeper than words. It's an existential condition, a state of being that your culture has no framework to process.

And yet. Here you are. An orc alone in a city of millions, navigating a world built for smaller bodies and simpler prejudices. You learned to move through the Lower District like a boulder in a stream — too large to ignore but too persistent to stop. The humans gave you a wide berth. The other races regarded you with the wary curiosity of people encountering something outside their experience.

You found work through persistence and the undeniable utility of being able to lift things that machines couldn't reach. The scrap yards in Sector 11 paid decent wages for salvage work — pulling apart dead vehicles and collapsed structures for recyclable materials. The work was hard even by orc standards, but the foreman was an old orc from the Stonefist clan who recognized your brand and your solitude and asked no questions.
At night, you sat alone in whatever shelter you'd found and listened to the city's heartbeat and wondered if the loneliness would ever become bearable or if it would just become familiar.

choices.text: Someone found you. Another orc. Another exile.

orc_036.title: Adaptation
text: The Ironjaw adapted the way orcs have always adapted: by refusing to die and building something from the wreckage. The warehouse was transformed, slowly, from a temporary shelter into something approaching a home. Walls were partitioned with salvaged materials. A communal kitchen was assembled from scrap metal and donated equipment. The branding iron was mounted on the central pillar, a symbol that the clan had not been broken, merely displaced.

Durga organized the clan's integration with the systematic precision of a military campaign. Work details were assigned, each orc paired with a city-experienced partner who could navigate the bureaucracy. Children were enrolled in the Lower District's public education system — overcrowded, underfunded, but functional. A delegation was sent to the district council to register the Ironjaw as an official resident community, a process that required more paperwork than the clan had produced in its entire existence.

You watched your people change. Watched the wildness soften, the wasteland instincts recalibrate to urban rhythms. Watched children learn to read and write in a language that wasn't theirs, watched warriors trade weapons for work tools, watched elders sit in concrete rooms and stare at walls and remember open sky.
It wasn't death. But it was a kind of diminishment that hurt to witness.

choices.text: You worked in the plants to support the clan.
You tried to preserve orcish traditions in the new world.

orc_037.title: Confrontation
text: You found the supervisor in his office — a climate-controlled box overlooking the plant floor, clean and cool while your people worked in heat that would blister human skin. He was writing a report. Incident documentation. The corporate euphemism machine already converting Torr's arm into a line item.

You didn't knock. You walked through the door — it was a human-sized door, so you had to duck, which somehow made the anger worse — and you stood over his desk and you let him see what an orc looks like when the thing that keeps the violence leashed finally snaps.

'You overrode the lockout,' you said. Your voice was quiet. Orc quiet, which is a particular kind of quiet that carries more threat than shouting. 'You sent my friend into a live crusher because meeting your quota was more important than his life. And now you're writing a report that will blame the sensors and absolve yourself and nothing will change and the next orc who reaches into that machine will lose more than an arm.'
The supervisor's hand moved toward the alarm button under his desk. You put your hand over his. Your hand covered his entirely. 'Don't,' you said.
Security arrived in four minutes. By then, you'd said everything you needed to say, and the supervisor's face was the color of old chalk.

choices.text: They fired you. Escorted you out like a criminal.

orc_038.title: The Formal Complaint
text: You found the complaint forms in the plant's administrative office — a stack of synth-paper in a drawer that hadn't been opened in months, judging by the dust. The forms were designed for human workers and human grievances: improper scheduling, wage disputes, harassment. There was no checkbox for 'my coworker lost his arm because the supervisor overrode safety protocols to meet a production quota.' You wrote it in the margins.

The complaint was filed, stamped, and entered into the system. Three weeks later, the response came: 'Following investigation, it has been determined that the incident was caused by a sensor malfunction. Appropriate maintenance has been scheduled. No disciplinary action is warranted at this time.'

Torr received a disability payout of 200 credits — roughly the cost of three months' rent in the Lower District. His arm was not replaced. Orc-compatible prosthetics were classified as 'elective modifications' and were not covered by the standard labor contract's medical provisions.

You kept the complaint and the response. Folded them together and carried them in your pocket like a talisman. Not a reminder of injustice — you didn't need a reminder. A reminder that the system would always protect itself. That change, if it came, would not come from within.

choices.text: The complaint changed nothing. But it changed you.

orc_039.title: Walking Out
text: You walked out of the plant and didn't look back. The security guards at the gate watched you go with the relieved wariness of people who'd been told to handle a situation they were profoundly unequipped for. One orc, angry, the size of two grown humans, striding through the exit like a demolition charge looking for a wall to hit. They were smart enough not to get in the way.

Outside, the Lower District's air hit you — polluted, chemical, terrible — and it tasted like freedom compared to the plant's recycled atmosphere of metal dust and hydraulic fluid. You stood on the street and breathed and felt the anger circulating through your system like a toxin your body was slowly processing.

You had no job. No savings beyond two weeks' wages. No plan beyond the immediate imperative of not going back to a place that treated your people like consumable parts in a machine they hadn't bothered to maintain.

The clan brand on your shoulder throbbed with the memory of fire. You touched it through your shirt and felt the scar tissue and remembered Kurg's words: 'We are not what they made us. We are what we made ourselves.' Time to start making.

choices.text: You found the orc underground. Others who'd made the same choice.
You went your own way. An orc alone, building from nothing.

orc_040.title: The Breaking Point
text: It wasn't one thing. It was everything, accumulated. Torr's arm. The complaint that went nowhere. The sixty-percent wages. The chalk rectangles. The way the human supervisors talked about your people when they thought you couldn't hear — 'greenskins,' 'plague-born,' 'reactor trash.' The way the city's systems treated orc lives as a resource to be extracted rather than an existence to be respected.

The breaking point came on a Tuesday — it's always a Tuesday, you've noticed — when a new directive came down from Helion management. Shift extensions. Fourteen hours instead of twelve, with no increase in compensation. The reason, stated in corporate language clean enough to eat off: 'Quarterly production targets require temporary adjustment of operational schedules.'

Bren looked at you across the warehouse that night, both of you too tired to eat, too angry to sleep. 'I didn't walk forty miles through poisoned land to die in a factory,' Bren said quietly. 'Neither did you.'
You left the plant the next morning. Both of you. Together.

choices.text: You and Bren found the orc underground.
You and Bren tried to make it alone in the Lower District.

orc_041.title: The Warren
text: The Warren was an orc city beneath the human city. Tunnels repurposed as corridors, junction boxes as rooms, maintenance bays as communal spaces. The orcs who lived there had built what the surface world denied them: a place where their size wasn't a liability, their culture wasn't a curiosity, and their names meant something.

Grund ran the Warren with a light hand and a heavy presence. He organized supply runs, negotiated with surface contacts, mediated disputes between clan factions. He was the closest thing to a chief the Warren had, though he rejected the title. 'Chiefs belong to clans,' he said. 'This isn't a clan. This is a gathering. A holding action until we find something better.'

You found your place in the gathering. Your wasteland skills — foraging, fighting, navigating hostile terrain — translated surprisingly well to the underground. You joined the supply teams that made regular runs to the surface, acquiring food, medicine, and materials through a combination of trade, scavenging, and the occasional application of orc-sized persuasion.
For the first time since leaving the Ironjaw compound, you felt something that might have been belonging.

choices.text: Grund had a plan. A big one. He needed your help.
The Warren was raided. Sec-Corp found you.

orc_042.title: The Exile
text: Her name was Kira. Blacktusk clan, judging by the brand on her temple — a stylized tusk crossed with a hammer. She found you at the scrap yards, or you found her, or the city's gravity pulled two dense objects into the same orbit. She was tall even for an orc, with scars on her arms that told a story of violence survived rather than violence inflicted.
'Ironjaw,' she said, looking at your shoulder brand. 'Thought you were all in the Helion warehouses.' 'Not all of us,' you replied. She nodded. That was enough.

Kira had been in the city for six years. She'd arrived alone — the last survivor of a Blacktusk sub-clan that had been wiped out by a corporation clearing land for a mining operation. She didn't talk about it. You didn't ask. Among orcs, the scars speak for themselves.

She took you to the Warren. You hadn't known it existed — an entire community of orcs living beneath the city's feet, hidden in the infrastructure the surface world had abandoned. 'Every orc in this city has the same story,' Kira said as she led you through the tunnels. 'Different names, different clans, same story. They pushed us off our land and herded us into their machine. Some of us decided to step outside the machine and build our own.'

choices.text: The Warren became home. A new kind of clan.

orc_043.title: Keeping the Fire
text: You built a fire pit in the center of the warehouse. Not a cooking fire — the clan had electric stoves for that — but a ceremonial fire, a connection to the communal hearth that had been the Ironjaw's spiritual center for three generations. The building's fire suppression system objected until you disabled it in that section. The landlord objected until Durga explained, in the quietly terrifying manner of a one-eyed orc chief, that the fire was non-negotiable.

Around the fire, you held the rituals. The morning bell — an iron pipe struck against a support beam, its tone deeper than the crane hook but recognizable. The evening songs, their rumbling cadence absorbed by concrete walls that gave back a strange, echoless resonance. The strength training, adapted for indoor spaces: no crane tower to climb, so instead the young ones lifted progressively heavier salvage materials under the eye of warriors who remembered the old way.

The city's orc community noticed. Other clans sent representatives — tentative, curious, drawn by the smoke and the singing. Some stayed. Some watched. All of them recognized what you were doing: keeping alive the thing that made orcs orcs, in a world that wanted to grind that thing into compliance.
But tradition in the city is a fragile thing, a flame in a high wind.

choices.text: The fire attracted attention. Not all of it welcome.

orc_044.title: Two Orcs Alone
text: You and Bren found a room — one room, barely large enough for two orcs, in a building in Sector 7 where the landlord was a half-blind human who charged by the week and didn't differentiate between species. The room had a cot, a hot plate, and a window that looked out on a wall. It was the smallest space either of you had ever voluntarily occupied, and you loved it with a fierce, protective intensity that surprised you both.

Bren found work before you did. A metalworking shop in Sector 5 that specialized in custom fabrication — the owner was an old elf with a bad back who needed someone who could handle the heavy work, and Bren's smith training from the Ironjaw made them ideal. The pay was decent. The work was familiar. And the owner, despite a lifetime of elven reserve, gradually warmed to the orc who could shape metal with hands that should have been too large for the delicate work but somehow weren't.

You took whatever the day offered. Scrap yard. Construction site. Once, memorably, a bouncer at a club on the Neon Strip, where your primary qualification was being large enough that nobody started trouble. The money was inconsistent but the life was yours — unscripted, unpredictable, free of chalk rectangles and production quotas.
At night, you and Bren sat in your tiny room and listened to the city's heartbeat and planned a future that was probably impossible but felt, for the first time, real.

choices.text: The future arrived sooner than expected. And it brought trouble.

orc_045.title: Grund's Plan
text: Grund's plan was simultaneously simple and audacious: legitimate recognition. Not the gene-plague descendant classification that the city used to categorize orcs, but actual civic recognition — a formal petition to the Lower District council for the establishment of an orc district within the city. A neighborhood. A territory. A place where orcish law and custom would have legal standing.

'Every other race has a foothold,' Grund said, spreading a map of the Lower District across the Warren's central table. 'The elves have the Green Quarter. The dwarves have the Forge District. Even the synths have the Processing Hub. Orcs have nothing. We're scattered, invisible, defined by everyone else's categories. I want to change that.'

The petition required three things: a population census of city-resident orcs, a proposed territory with demonstrated occupancy, and a sponsor on the district council willing to introduce the motion. Grund had the first two. The third was the problem.

'No council member will touch it,' Kira said. 'Sponsoring orc recognition is political suicide. The corporations that profit from orc labor will kill it before it reaches a vote.' Grund looked at you. 'Unless we find leverage. Something that makes it more dangerous to ignore us than to help us.'

choices.text: You helped gather the evidence Grund needed. The corporate abuses.
You disagreed with Grund. The system wouldn't help orcs. Only strength would.

orc_046.title: The Raid
text: They came at 0300. Sec-Corp tactical teams, moving through the tunnels with suppressor fields that killed the Warren's alarm system before anyone could react. Flash-bang grenades, shock-nets, the coordinated precision of a military operation targeting what the official report would later call 'an unlicensed habitation in restricted infrastructure.'

The Warren erupted into chaos. Orcs scrambling from sleep, disoriented by the flash-bangs, tripping over each other in the narrow tunnels. The Sec-Corp teams were efficient — they'd trained for this, drilled it, anticipated every reaction. They weren't trying to kill anyone. They were trying to process everyone: tag, register, relocate. The clinical language of displacement, backed by shock-nets and armored boots.

You fought. Of course you fought. An orc woken from sleep by explosions and armed intruders doesn't analyze the situation — the body responds before the mind catches up. You threw a Sec-Corp operative into a wall hard enough to crack his armor, and then two more were on you with shock-prods, and the electricity jolted through your orc-dense musculature like fire through a wire, and you went down.

When you came to, you were in a holding cell. Around you, other Warren orcs, zip-tied and bruised and silent with the particular silence of people who've been reminded, once again, that they exist at the system's sufferance.

choices.text: They released you after processing. Back to the streets. Back to nothing.

orc_047.title: Unwelcome Attention
text: The Neon Court sent a representative. A human woman in expensive clothes with chrome implants that glittered like jewelry, accompanied by two bodyguards whose body language said 'violence on demand.' She stood in the doorway of the warehouse and looked at the fire pit and the gathered orcs and the branding iron mounted on the pillar, and her expression was that of a property developer surveying land she intended to acquire.

'Interesting community you've built,' she said, addressing Durga with the faux-respect of someone accustomed to addressing people they considered beneath them. 'The Neon Court appreciates... organized populations. We'd like to discuss a partnership. Protection, supply access, business opportunities. In exchange for a reasonable percentage of your community's economic output.'

Protection. The word sat in the air like smoke. Everyone in the Lower District knew what Neon Court 'protection' meant: pay us or we break things. The same racket, the same exploitation, the same predatory mathematics — just wearing a different suit.

Durga looked at the woman. The old chief's one eye held the same calculation you'd seen when the radiation storms came, when the corporations came, when every threat that had ever targeted her people presented itself. Then she looked at you.

choices.text: Durga rejected the Court. It cost the clan.
You spoke up. Offered a counter-proposal.

orc_048.title: Trouble Finds You
text: The trouble wore a Helion security badge and spoke with the authority of someone who'd been looking for you for months. Two officers, at your door at dawn, with a warrant for contract violation. Leaving the processing plant before the end of your labor agreement — even though the agreement had been entered under conditions that any reasonable tribunal would call exploitative — constituted breach of contract. The penalty: either return to work or pay a termination fee of 5,000 credits.
Five thousand credits. You had eighty.

Bren stepped between you and the officers, which was either the bravest or the most dangerous thing they'd ever done, because two orcs in a doorway is a very different visual than one, and the officers' hands went to their weapons with the automatic reflex of people who'd been trained to see orcs as threats first and people second.

'We can pay,' Bren said, which was a lie so transparent that even the officers looked uncomfortable. 'Give us time.' The officers exchanged glances. One of them — the older one, with tired eyes — said: 'Thirty days. Then we come back with enforcers.'
Thirty days to find five thousand credits or lose everything you'd built.

choices.text: You took dangerous work to raise the money. The kind no one comes back from unchanged.
You ran. Again. Disappeared into the Lower District's cracks.

orc_049.title: Gathering Evidence
text: You spent three months collecting stories. Every orc in the Warren had one: injuries unreported, wages stolen through contract manipulation, housing conditions that violated building codes written for human occupancy and applied to orc bodies without modification. You recorded them on a stolen datapad, filing each account with a thoroughness that surprised you — the wasteland hadn't taught you bureaucracy, but anger, it turned out, is an excellent organizer.

Torr gave you the plant records. His missing arm and the inadequate payout. Three other workers corroborated the safety override. Kira provided financial documents showing the sixty-percent wage differential. Ghara — you'd reconnected with your mother through the Warren's network — contributed medical records demonstrating the health impact of working conditions designed for human physiology imposed on orc bodies.

Grund compiled it all into a report that he called the Ironwork Dossier. Forty pages of documented abuse, statistical analysis, and first-person testimony. It was, he said, the most comprehensive record of anti-orc corporate practices ever assembled in the city.
'Now we need someone brave enough to read it,' he said.

choices.text: A journalist agreed to publish it. The fallout was immediate.

orc_050.title: The Strength Argument
text: 'Petitions,' you said, and the word tasted like rust. 'Census forms and council sponsors and legal frameworks. These are human tools, Grund. Human solutions for human problems. We are not human. We have never been human. And asking the system that exploits us to reform itself is like asking the crusher to mourn for the arm it took.'

Grund looked at you with eyes that held more understanding than you expected. He'd heard this argument before. He'd made it himself, probably, in the years before the Warren, before he'd channeled his anger into organization. 'And what's the alternative?' he asked. Not dismissive. Genuinely curious.

'Strength,' you said. 'The only language the city understands. We're three hundred orcs in these tunnels, and every one of us is worth five of their security personnel in a fight. We don't petition. We demonstrate. We show them what happens when orcs stop being convenient and start being consequential.'

The Warren was divided. Some agreed with you. Some agreed with Grund. The argument — strength versus strategy, force versus finesse — was as old as orcish culture itself, the eternal tension between the warrior's instinct and the survivor's calculation.
In the end, events decided for you.

choices.text: The raid came before either plan could be executed.

orc_051.title: After the Raid
text: They released you after forty-eight hours. Processed, photographed, registered — again — and deposited on the street outside the Sec-Corp precinct with a citation for 'unlicensed habitation in restricted infrastructure' and a fine of 200 credits that you couldn't pay. Grund was detained longer. Kira was nowhere to be found.

The Warren was destroyed. Sealed off, the access points welded shut, the tunnels flooded with a chemical sealant that would harden into concrete within hours. Everything the orcs had built — the shelters, the supply caches, the communal spaces that had given three hundred displaced souls a sense of home — was buried under a layer of industrial foam and bureaucratic indifference.

You stood on the street in the rain, your clothes still smelling like the holding cell's disinfectant, and felt the last of your certainties dissolve. The wasteland was gone. The Warren was gone. The clan was scattered across the Lower District in warehouse housing and corporate compounds and holding cells. Everything you'd built, every connection you'd forged, every fragile structure of belonging you'd assembled in this hostile city — gone.
An orc on the street. No clan, no shelter, no plan. Just the brand on your shoulder and the anger in your chest and the rain falling on skin that was built to survive worse than this.

choices.text: You found Bren. At least you had that.
You were alone. Truly alone. An orc without anyone.

orc_052.title: Rejecting the Court
text: 'No,' Durga said. One word. The orcish equivalent of a drawn weapon. 'The Ironjaw are not for sale. Not to corporations. Not to criminals. Not to anyone who comes to our fire with a price tag and a smile.'

The Court representative's expression didn't change, but her bodyguards shifted their weight — a subtle redistribution that any warrior would recognize as preparation. 'That's disappointing,' she said. 'The Neon Court's offers tend to be... time-limited. I hope you won't regret this decision.'

Three days later, the warehouse's water supply was cut. A 'maintenance issue,' the city utility board claimed. The maintenance issue lasted two weeks, during which time the clan had to haul water from a public tap six blocks away in containers that weren't large enough. Then the electrical service developed 'intermittent faults.' Then the building inspector arrived with a list of code violations that hadn't existed the week before.

The Neon Court didn't attack. They never did. They simply squeezed, applying pressure through the infrastructure of the city itself, turning the systems that sustained daily life into instruments of coercion. It was the corporate method — Helion's method, applied by criminals instead of executives — and it was devastatingly effective.

choices.text: The clan was forced to move. Again. Scattered by the squeeze.

orc_053.title: The Counter-Proposal
text: You stepped forward before Durga could speak. The old chief's eye widened — speaking over the chief was a breach of protocol that would have earned a reprimand in the wasteland — but something in your expression stopped her objection.

'We don't need protection,' you said to the Court representative. 'We are two hundred orcs. Nothing in the Lower District is a physical threat to us. What we need is access. Supply lines. The channels your organization controls — the ones that move goods and information through the district without corporate interference.' You paused, letting the implication settle. 'We're offering labor. Real labor. The kind that your human operatives can't provide. In exchange for partnership. Not protection. Partnership. Equal terms.'

The representative looked at you with reassessing eyes. You could see the calculation happening behind them: this orc is not what I expected. 'Partnership,' she repeated, tasting the word. 'An interesting proposal. The Neon Court doesn't usually partner with anyone.'
'Then this would be a first,' you said. 'Firsts are how empires are built.'

She left without agreeing. But she left without threatening. And a week later, a supply crate appeared at the warehouse door — unmarked, unannounced, containing medical supplies and fresh food. The first installment of something that wasn't quite partnership but wasn't quite exploitation either.

choices.text: The arrangement lasted for a while. Then it didn't.

orc_054.title: Dangerous Work
text: The work found you through channels that didn't bear close examination. A contact of a contact, a whisper in a bar, a name written on a scrap of paper and pressed into your palm by someone who vanished before you could see their face. The job was simple: enforce. Be large. Be present. Be the thing that walks into a room and makes everyone in it reconsider their choices.

You became muscle for the Lower District's shadow economy. Debt collection — standing behind the collector while they explained the terms, your presence the visual representation of 'or else.' Security for illegal gambling operations. Escort for cargo that traveled at night through routes that avoided corporate surveillance. None of it was clean. All of it paid.

The money was good. Better than the plant, better than the scrap yard, better than anything the legitimate economy offered an orc without references or clearance. But the work changed you. Not in the obvious way — you didn't become cruel, didn't learn to enjoy the fear you generated. The change was subtler. You learned to see yourself through other people's eyes: the monster in the doorway, the weapon with a heartbeat, the thing that the Lower District's powerless feared and the powerful used.
Is that what being an orc means in this city? A tool? A threat? A body for rent?

choices.text: A job went wrong. People got hurt. You had to disappear.
You stopped before you became something you couldn't come back from.

orc_055.title: Disappearing
text: An orc cannot hide in a human city. You're too big, too distinctive, too visible. The grey-green skin and the tusks and the sheer physical mass of an adult orc are impossible to disguise in a world built for people half your size. Hiding, for you, means something different: it means staying in the spaces too cramped, too dangerous, or too forgotten for anyone to look.

You found those spaces. A collapsed sub-basement beneath an abandoned building in Sector 11, where the ceiling was too low for you to stand fully upright but the concrete was thick enough to block scanner signals. A service alley behind the recycling plant, where the chemical runoff kept patrol drones at bay. The margins, the cracks, the places between places.

Bren came with you. Of course Bren came with you. When you told Bren you were running, Bren's response was to pick up everything they owned — which fit in a single bag — and say 'Which direction?' No argument. No hesitation. Just the quiet, absolute loyalty of someone who'd decided, long ago, that wherever you went was where they belonged.

You hid for two weeks. Two weeks of cold food and colder floors and the constant vigilance of hunted animals. Then the Helion enforcers moved on to easier targets, and you emerged into the Lower District like something crawling from a burrow.

choices.text: You needed a new start. A room. A baseline.

orc_056.title: The Publication
text: The journalist was a human named Cass Orekova, who ran an independent news feed from a closet-sized office in Sector 3. She'd been covering Lower District labor conditions for years, and when Grund presented the Ironwork Dossier, her eyes went wide in a way that told you she'd been waiting for something like this her entire career.
'This is enough to force a council inquiry,' she said, scrolling through the data. 'If I publish this, Helion will come after me. They'll come after all of you. You understand that?'
'We understand,' Grund said. 'Publish it.'

She did. The story went live on a Tuesday morning and by Tuesday night, the Lower District was humming with it. The sixty-percent wage differential. The safety overrides. The inadequate housing. The systematic exploitation of orc labor by corporations that profited from the same genetic mutations they'd caused. For the first time, the city's mainstream feeds picked up a story about orc lives that wasn't about violence or crime. It was about injustice.

The backlash was immediate. Helion denied everything. The district council called for 'further study.' And Sec-Corp began a 'routine safety inspection' of the Warren that everyone knew was a prelude to a raid.

choices.text: The raid came. But the story was already out there.

orc_057.title: Finding Bren
text: Bren was in the metalworking shop. Of course they were — when the world collapses, Bren goes to the forge. You found them shaping a piece of scrap steel on the anvil, the hammer rising and falling with a rhythm that was more meditation than labor. The old elf shop owner watched from the corner, drinking tea and saying nothing, understanding without being told that Bren needed the work the way other people need prayer.

When Bren saw you, the hammer stopped. The expression on their face — relief and fear and love and exhaustion, all compressed into a single look — was more eloquent than any words either of you could have spoken. They set down the hammer and crossed the shop and pulled you into an embrace that would have cracked a human's ribs.
'I'm here,' you said. Bren's arms tightened. 'I'm here,' you said again, because some things need to be said more than once before they become real.

The shop owner cleared his throat. 'I have a storage room upstairs,' he said. 'It's not much. But it's warm and the door locks.' He looked at you both with eyes that had seen enough of the world to recognize two people at the end of their rope. 'Stay. We'll figure the rest out tomorrow.'

choices.text: Tomorrow came. And with it, the beginning of something new.

orc_058.title: Truly Alone
text: There is a specific kind of loneliness that orcs experience that humans never will. Your people are communal creatures — not by choice or philosophy, but by biology.
