The orc nervous system is wired for the presence of others: the low-frequency vibrations of nearby orc heartbeats, the pheromonal signatures that communicate mood and health, the subsonic rumble of a group at rest. When those signals are absent, the orc body responds with something akin to withdrawal — a physical ache, a hollow thrumming in the chest, a wrongness that no amount of food or sleep can resolve.

You felt it now. Alone on the streets of the Lower District, surrounded by millions of people and none of them yours, the hollowness spread through you like cold through iron. You walked and the walking didn't help. You ate and the food was ash. You slept and woke to the silence where your clan's heartbeat should have been.

Ghara was somewhere in the city. Bren too, maybe, if they hadn't been picked up in the raid. The clan was scattered — not destroyed, you told yourself, scattered — and the Ironjaw brand on your shoulder burned with the memory of a fire that still existed somewhere, in someone, even if you couldn't feel its heat.
You are an orc. You were built to endure. But endurance without community is just a slower way of being empty.

choices.text: You kept going. Because stopping was the only thing worse than the loneliness.

orc_059.title: The Job That Went Wrong
text: The job was supposed to be simple. Stand outside a door. Look menacing. Wait for the signal. Go home. But the person on the other side of the door wasn't a debtor — they were a former Neon Court operative with a modified weapon and a death wish, and when the door opened it wasn't to negotiate.

The blast caught you in the shoulder. A chemical round — the kind that burns through orc skin, which is saying something because orc skin can resist most conventional weapons. The pain was white-hot and immediate, and the rage that followed was the deep, ancestral rage of an animal that has been hurt and will now hurt something in return.

You don't remember clearly what happened next. The reports said property damage, which was a euphemism for 'an orc went through three walls and a support pillar.' Nobody died, which was the only reason you weren't facing a murder charge. But the operative was in critical condition and the property owner was demanding compensation and the person who'd hired you had vanished the way people in the shadow economy always vanish: completely, instantly, as if they'd never existed.

You ran. Shoulder still bleeding, the chemical burn eating through your shirt, you ran through the Neon Strip's back alleys until the neon signs faded and the Lower District's familiar darkness closed around you like a hand.

choices.text: You went to ground. Found a hole. Waited for the heat to pass.

orc_060.title: The Line
text: The job was a collection. A family in Sector 4 — a human family, a mother and two children — who owed money to someone who hired you to remind them of that fact. You stood outside their door and heard the children inside, heard one of them ask their mother why the big green person was standing outside, and something inside you — something older than the city, older than the exploitation, older than the gene-plague — said no.

Not this. Not children. The wasteland had taken everything from you except the core of what Kurg and Tohma and Ghara had built into your identity: the understanding that strength without conscience is just destruction wearing a name. You were an orc. You were not a weapon.

You walked away from the door. The person who'd hired you called the next day, angry. You told them you were done. They threatened. You said: 'I am an orc. I have survived reactor fires and radiation storms and the death of my home. Your threats are wind.' Then you hung up.
The money stopped. The dangerous work stopped. You were back to nothing, which was starting to feel like a familiar address.

choices.text: Back to nothing. But at least the nothing was clean.

orc_061.title: Sector 7
text: Sector 7 of the Lower District is where things go to settle. Not people — things. The sediment of the city's churning population, the residue of lives disrupted and displaced and rearranged by forces beyond individual control. The buildings are old, the infrastructure neglected, the community a patchwork of humans and orcs and synths and elves and every other variety of sentient being that the upper city has discarded.

You found the boarding house on a side street off the main corridor. The building was three stories of concrete and resignation, with a landlord who was either very tolerant or very indifferent — an old woman who looked at your orc frame and your tired eyes and your empty pockets and said: 'Room 7C. Rent's due weekly. Don't break anything.'

The room was too small for you. Of course it was. Every room in the Lower District was too small for an orc. The cot sagged under your weight. The ceiling was low enough that your head brushed it when you stood straight. The window looked out on a wall so close you could touch it.

But the door locked. And the walls, thin as they were, were solid enough to create the illusion of separation between you and the world that had been grinding against you since the day you left the wasteland.

choices.text: Sit on the cot. Feel the springs bend. Breathe.
You found your mother. Ghara is alive.

orc_062.title: The Cot
text: The cot groans under you like a living thing in pain. You sit carefully — orc careful, which means adjusting your weight with the delicacy of someone who's broken too many human-designed objects to be careless. The springs settle. The frame holds. You take a breath that fills the entire small room and let it out slowly, and the exhalation carries something you can't name — not relief, not grief, something between the two, something that belongs to a category of emotion that only exists at the intersection of exhaustion and arrival.

Your shoulder aches where the clan brand sits beneath your shirt. You pull the collar aside and look at it — the Ironjaw symbol, the interlocking teeth, seared into grey-green skin by white-hot iron and Elder Kurg's steady hand. It's the only thing from the wasteland that you still carry. The only physical proof that everything that came before — the compound, the clan, the trials, the songs — was real and not a dream cooked up by a lonely mind in a too-small room.

You touch the brand with your fingertips. The scar tissue is smooth and hard, like the calluses on your palms, like the carapace of resilience your people have built around themselves over generations. You are Ironjaw. You are orc. Whatever this city takes from you, it cannot take that.

choices.text: Look out the window. See this world you've been dropped into.
Close your eyes. Remember the wasteland one more time.

orc_063.title: The Window
text: The window is too small for your face. You have to angle your head to see through it, pressing one cheek against the cold glass while your tusks click against the frame. Outside: the alley, the opposite wall, a strip of sky above that shows nothing but the bottoms of higher buildings and the faint glow of the upper city's lights reflecting off the permanent cloud cover.

In the wasteland, the sky was vast. Poisoned and dangerous and frequently deadly, but vast. Open in a way that this city will never be. You could stand on the crane tower and see to the horizon in every direction, and the emptiness, however hostile, at least had the honesty of being empty. Here, the compression of space and people and concrete creates a different kind of emptiness — the emptiness of being surrounded and still alone.

Below the window, the alley is occupied. A human vendor is setting up a food cart, wrestling with a canopy in the pre-dawn dark. A synth walks past with the measured stride of someone conserving battery. A cat — an actual cat, gene-modified to survive the chemical atmosphere — sits on a dumpster and watches everything with green eyes that glow in the dark.
This is your world now. These walls, this alley, these strangers. The wasteland is behind you, and the city stretches ahead like a labyrinth made of steel and indifference.

choices.text: Step away from the window. Look at what you have.

orc_064.title: The Wasteland Memory
text: You close your eyes and let the memory come. Not a specific memory — the whole of it. The compound. The crane tower. The fire pit with its circle of amber eyes. The sound of the evening songs vibrating through concrete and bone. The smell of irradiated earth after rain — a sharp, mineral scent that you'll never smell again but will never forget.

Kurg's voice: 'We are not what they made us.' Tohma's hand on your face: 'You are the proof.' Ghara's steady fingers setting a fracture, her voice soft: 'Hold still, child. This will hurt, but it will heal.' Bren on the crane tower, pointing at the city's lights: 'What do you think it's like?' And your answer, young and bold and ignorant: 'We'll find out.'

You found out. The city is like the reactor: it contains you, it uses you, and it doesn't care what you become inside its walls. But you are still here. You are still orc. The radiation couldn't kill your ancestors, the wasteland couldn't kill your clan, and this city — this vast, grinding, indifferent machine of human civilization — will not kill you.
You open your eyes. The room is small and shabby and yours. The brand on your shoulder pulses with remembered warmth.

choices.text: Enough remembering. Look at what you have. Start from here.

orc_065.title: Taking Stock
text: What you have: a room. A body. A brand. The clothes on your back. A handful of credits that will cover rent for two weeks if you eat sparingly. The knowledge of how to fight, how to endure, how to survive in conditions that would kill anything softer. The name you claimed at the fire, spoken into the silence of a clan that no longer gathers.

What you don't have: a clan. A purpose. A plan. An understanding of how this city works beyond the crude mechanics of exploitation you've experienced firsthand. The comforting presence of other orc heartbeats in your chest. The assurance that tomorrow will be better than today.

On the metal shelf, wedged into a corner, someone has scratched words into the surface with a sharp object. You lean close to read them. They're in orcish — the crude, hybrid language your people developed in the reactors, part human speech, part something new. Most city dwellers can't read it. You can.

The scratched words say: 'Jaw still strong.' The Ironjaw motto. Carved by an orc who lived in this room before you, who sat on this cot and looked at these walls and felt the same hollowness and the same stubborn refusal to let it win.

choices.text: Jaw still strong. Yes. Always.

orc_066.title: The First Night
text: Night falls on the Lower District like a curtain of neon and shadow. Through your window, the alley's sodium lamp flickers to life, casting an orange glow that makes the opposite wall look like the inside of a furnace. The sounds change: the daytime hum of traffic and commerce gives way to the nighttime symphony of the Lower District — music from distant clubs, the occasional shout, the mechanical patrol of drones, the constant underlayer of rain on metal.

You lie on the cot and let the sounds wash over you. They're nothing like the wasteland's silence — the profound, radioactive quiet of a landscape where the loudest sound was wind through dead machinery. Here, the noise is alive, chaotic, human in a way that the wasteland never was. And buried in that noise, if you listen with the orc hearing that picks up frequencies humans can't perceive, you can hear other heartbeats. Through the walls, through the floor, through the building's skeleton of steel and concrete — the heartbeats of your neighbors, of the people sleeping and waking and living in the spaces adjacent to yours.

They're not orc heartbeats. They don't carry the deep, resonant thrum that your nervous system craves. But they're alive. And in the silence where your clan's heartbeat should be, even human hearts are better than nothing.

choices.text: Sleep. Dream of the wasteland, dream of the city.
Listen to the rain. Find beauty in the noise.

orc_067.title: The Dream
text: You dream of fire. Not the destructive kind — the communal kind. The Ironjaw fire pit, burning bright in the center of the compound, two hundred orcs gathered around it in a circle of muscle and bone and shared survival. The evening songs rising from two hundred throats, a sound so deep and resonant that it makes the ground vibrate, that it turns the compound into a single enormous instrument playing a note that has never been played before and will never be played again.

In the dream, you are standing at the fire's edge. Kurg is there, whole and alive, his ancient eyes reflecting the flames. Tohma is there, her blind eyes turned toward the heat. Ghara is there, her healer's hands resting in her lap. Bren is there, standing beside you, close enough that your shoulders touch.
'Remember,' Kurg says. But it's not a command. It's a blessing. Remember this. Remember what you are. Remember where you came from. Carry it with you into the place where you're going.

You wake to grey light and rain on the window and the ache of a body pressed into a space too small for it. But the dream lingers — the warmth, the sound, the feeling of being surrounded by your people — and you hold onto it the way you hold onto the brand, the way you hold onto the scratched motto on the shelf: tightly, fiercely, with everything you have.

choices.text: Get up. Face the city. Show it what an orc can do.

orc_068.title: The First Morning
text: You rise and the room contracts around you like a fist. Ceiling too low. Walls too close. Door frame too narrow — you'll have to turn sideways to pass through it, the way you've learned to turn sideways through every door in this city that wasn't built for you. The mirror on the wall is hung at a height designed for human faces; you have to crouch to see yourself in it.

The face that looks back is yours and not yours. The same grey-green skin, the same amber eyes, the same tusks curving from the lower jaw. But there's something different in the expression — something the wasteland version of you wouldn't recognize. Not defeat. Not surrender. Something more like the expression of a person who has been beaten and burned and broken and displaced and exploited, and is still, despite everything, standing.

You straighten up. Your horns scrape the ceiling, leaving fresh marks alongside old ones — the marks of previous orc tenants, the archaeological record of every orc who's stood in this room and refused to shrink.
Your stomach growls. Time to eat. Time to work. Time to begin the daily business of proving to a city that doesn't believe it that orc lives have value.

choices.text: Head to the street. Find food. Find work.
The orc in the mirror stands tall.

orc_069.title: The Street
text: The Lower District's morning rush is a river of bodies flowing through channels designed for half their number. You step into the current and immediately displace it — the crowd parts around you the way water parts around a boulder, an automatic response to your size and your species that is part practical (you take up a lot of space) and part prejudice (an orc in the crowd changes the crowd's emotional calculation).

The food vendors are open. A noodle cart, a synth-fruit stand, a woman selling protein bars from a tray hanging from her neck. You buy noodles — they're cheap and hot and the vendor, an old orc from the Stonefist clan, gives you an extra serving without being asked. Clan recognition. The silent solidarity of your people in a world that would prefer you didn't exist.

The job board at the transit hub glows with listings. Most of them are coded in ways you're learning to read: 'Heavy labor' means orc-appropriate. 'Physical capability required' means they want muscle. 'No background check' means they don't care what you've done as long as you can lift things. The legitimate economy of orc employment in the Lower District, such as it is: lifting, carrying, breaking, building. The things machines can't do cheaply enough and humans can't do at all.
You scan the board. Something will turn up. Something always turns up. The city has an inexhaustible appetite for orc labor, even if it can't bring itself to pay fairly for it.

choices.text: Take a job. Start building. Jaw still strong.

orc_070.title: Day One
text: The job is construction. A building in Sector 6, going up fast — too fast, probably, but the contractor is on a deadline and the Lower District's building codes are more suggestion than law. Your role: carry steel beams from the staging area to the upper floors. Up six flights of temporary scaffolding, a beam on each shoulder, the metal biting into the skin through your shirt. Other species would use cranes. The contractor has an orc.

The work is familiar in its brutality. Your body responds to the physical demand the way it always has — efficiently, powerfully, without complaint. The muscles in your back and legs and arms engage with a fluid precision that makes the human workers stop and stare. Two beams per trip where they manage one. Six floors climbed while they're still on the third. You're not showing off. This is just what an orc body does.

At the end of the day, the foreman pays you in cash. Twenty credits for twelve hours. Less than the human workers make for lighter loads and shorter hours. The sixty-percent rule, applied informally even by employers who've never heard of the formal policy. It's just how things are done. An orc is worth sixty percent of a human. Everyone knows it. Nobody questions it.

Except you. You question it every day. You question it with every beam you carry, every flight you climb, every credit you earn. The question has no answer yet. But you keep asking it, because asking is how things change.

choices.text: Walk home. Eat. Sleep. Do it again tomorrow.

orc_071.title: The Walk Home
text: The walk from the construction site to Sector 7 takes forty minutes. You could ride the transit, but the transit costs credits you'd rather spend on food, and besides, the transit seats aren't built for orcs and the looks you get from human passengers range from nervous to hostile. So you walk, and the walking becomes its own ritual — a nightly passage through the Lower District's streets that teaches you the city's rhythms the way the wasteland taught you the radiation winds.

You learn the neighborhoods. Sector 6, where the construction is concentrated and the streets smell like wet concrete and welding flux. The market corridor between 6 and 7, where the vendors sell everything from synth-food to stolen tech under canopies of recycled plastic. The residential blocks of Sector 7, where the buildings lean against each other like tired commuters and the stairwells echo with the sounds of people living in spaces too small for their lives.

On the walk, you pass other orcs. A nod, a glance, the silent acknowledgment of shared experience. Sometimes a word — a greeting in the old tongue, spoken low, almost secret, a reminder that the language of the reactors is still alive in the mouths of those who carry it. These brief connections sustain you in ways you can't quantify. They're not the clan. Nothing will replace the clan. But they're proof that you're not the only one. That the fire, however scattered its embers, still burns.

choices.text: Home. The room. The cot. The beginning of the rest.

orc_072.title: The Threshold
text: You stand at the door of Room 7C and run your thumb over the Ironjaw scratching on the shelf — 'Jaw still strong' — and feel the truth of it in your bones. You are strong. Not just physically, though your body could still tear through a wall if the situation required it. Strong in the way that matters more: the strength that keeps you standing when everything in the world is built to push you down.

Behind you, the hallway. The building. The street. The city. The entire grinding apparatus of a civilization that created your people through negligence, exploited them through design, and now warehouses them in districts built for smaller bodies and simpler dreams. It's all still there. It will always be there.

But here, in this room — your room, claimed by right of survival and paid for in credits earned through labor that should be worth more — you are not a reactor worker's mutant offspring. You are not a gene-plague statistic. You are not a labor unit at sixty percent value. You are an orc. You are Ironjaw. You carry the fire.

You sit on the cot. The springs sing their familiar protest. You lean your head back until your horns press against the wall, adding new grooves to the collection left by every orc who's lived here before you. The gesture is unconscious, instinctive — marking territory, leaving proof of presence, the orc equivalent of writing your name on the world.

choices.text: Rest now. Tomorrow, everything changes.
The warrior rests before the next battle.
The bridge holds. Tomorrow you build more.

orc_073.title: Origin Complete
text: Sleep comes easier tonight. Not easy — sleep has never been easy, not in the wasteland's radiation-laced darkness, not in the city's neon-stained noise — but easier. Your body knows something your mind hasn't accepted yet: this is home now. This small, inadequate, stubbornly persistent room in the Lower District of a city that was never built for you.

You dream again, but differently this time. Not the wasteland dream, not the fire pit and the songs. This dream is new. You're walking through the Lower District, but the buildings are different — decorated with orcish symbols, the Ironjaw teeth and the Blacktusk hammer and the Stonefist mountain. The streets are wider, built for orc frames. The air smells like the communal fire, like forge smoke and roasted meat. And there are orcs everywhere — walking tall, heads up, taking up exactly as much space as they need.
It's a dream. Just a dream. But dreams are where the future starts, in the minds of people stubborn enough to imagine a world that doesn't exist yet and angry enough to build it.
You wake to rain and grey light and the sound of the city starting another day. You stand. Your horns scrape the ceiling. You don't duck.

The door is waiting. The city is waiting. And whatever comes next — the work, the struggle, the slow and furious business of building an orc life in a human world — you'll meet it the way your people have met everything: standing, unbroken, jaw still strong.

choices.text: Open the door. Step into the city. Begin.

orc_074.title: Ghara's Medicine
text: You found your mother in a basement clinic in Sector 4. Ghara had set up a practice — if you could call a folding table and a box of scavenged supplies a practice — treating orcs who could not afford the city's medical system. Which was all of them. The gene-plague descendant classification excluded orcs from the standard healthcare network, and the private clinics charged rates that a lifetime of sixty-percent wages could never cover.

She was thinner than you remembered. The city had worn her down the way the wasteland never could — not through physical hardship, which orcs were built to endure, but through the constant, grinding indignity of being treated as less. But her hands were still steady. Her eyes still held that fierce, maternal intelligence that saw through every lie and every wound to the truth beneath.

She looked at you — really looked, the way only a mother can — and said: 'You look tired.' And you said: 'I am tired.' And she nodded and said: 'Good. Tired means you are still fighting. The ones who stop being tired are the ones who have given up.'

choices.text: Ghara gave you something. Medicine for the soul.

orc_075.title: Medicine for the Soul
text: She gave you a pouch of dried lichen — the same irradiated lichen that grew on the reactor walls back in the compound, the same lichen she had used to treat burns and fevers and the hundred small injuries of wasteland life. It had no medicinal value in the city. The city had better drugs, synthesized in sterile labs by machines that had never touched contaminated soil.

But Ghara did not give you the lichen for its medicine. She gave it for its meaning. The smell of it — sharp, mineral, faintly luminescent — was the smell of home. Of the compound. Of the fire pit at dawn when the morning bell rang and the clan gathered and the songs began their low, chest-deep vibration.

You held the pouch to your nose and breathed in and the memory hit you like a physical force — the compound, the crane tower, the stars visible through breaks in the contamination clouds. For a moment you were there again, standing on the high ground with Bren beside you, looking at the city's distant lights and wondering what kind of future waited in that glow.
'Keep it,' Ghara said. 'When the city makes you forget what you are, open it. Breathe. Remember.'

choices.text: You kept the lichen. You kept the memory. You kept going.

orc_076.title: The Fight Pit
text: The fight pits on the Neon Strip were illegal, underground, and extremely profitable — for everyone except the fighters. The setup was simple: a concrete basement, a chalk circle, two combatants, and a crowd of humans who paid to watch the spectacle of bodies colliding. Orc fights were the most popular. The audience loved the scale, the power, the primal theater of two massive bodies locked in combat.

You fought because the money was immediate and the rules were simple. Win and you got paid. Lose and you got paid less. Die and you got nothing, but your body would be disposed of with an efficiency that the city's waste management system could envy.

The first fight was against a Stonefist orc named Brunn who outweighed you by fifty pounds and moved like the heavy machinery he probably operated during his day job. The crowd roared when you entered the pit. They roared louder when Brunn hit you hard enough to crack a rib. They roared loudest when you got back up.

You won. Not through superior skill or greater strength — Brunn had both — but through the thing Kurg had identified in the Trial of Iron: will. The willingness to keep standing after you should have fallen, to keep fighting after the pain says stop.

choices.text: The fights paid well but cost more than credits.

orc_077.title: The Cost of Fighting
text: After six fights, your body carried the inventory of violence: cracked ribs that healed at orc speed but ached in the rain, a split in your left tusk that would never fully close, scar tissue across your knuckles that turned your fists into something resembling geological formations. The money covered rent and food with enough left over to send credits to Ghara's clinic. The cost was measured in something the accountants could not quantify.

Each fight, the crowd saw an orc. A spectacle. An animal performing violence for their entertainment. They cheered your strength and feared your presence and never, not once, looked at you and saw a person. The pit reduced you to your body — to the grey-green skin and the tusks and the muscle — and the audience consumed that body the way they consumed everything in the Lower District: hungrily, wastefully, without gratitude.

After the sixth fight, you stood in the washroom behind the pit and looked at your reflection in a steel panel polished to a rough mirror and saw what they saw: a weapon. Not a warrior. Not an orc. A weapon. And weapons do not have names or histories or feelings. Weapons are used until they break.
You did not fight again.

choices.text: You walked away from the pit. Back to honest work, honest poverty.

orc_078.title: The Orcish Word
text: There is a word in the orcish language that has no human translation. Krug-mar. It means, approximately, the strength that comes from being broken. Not resilience — that implies bouncing back to an original form. Krug-mar is the understanding that the broken form is the stronger one. That the cracks, once healed, become the hardest parts. That the thing you are after being shattered is more durable than the thing you were before.

Your people invented this word in the reactors, when the radiation was breaking their DNA and rebuilding it into something new. The first generation of orcs looked at their changed bodies and their changed children and understood that they had been destroyed and remade, and that the remaking, however painful, however unwanted, had produced something the destroyers had not anticipated: something that could survive what killed everything else.

Krug-mar. You feel it now, sitting in Room 7C, your body aching from a day of labor, your mind heavy with the accumulated weight of displacement and exploitation and the slow, grinding work of building an orc life in a human city. You are broken. You have been broken repeatedly, by forces too large to fight and systems too complex to dismantle. But the breaks have healed, and the healed places are hard as iron, and the thing you have become is something no one — not Helion, not the Neon Court, not the city itself — anticipated or knows how to stop.

choices.text: Krug-mar. The strength of the broken. Your inheritance.

orc_079.title: Ending: The Warrior
text: You are a warrior. That is what the trials made you, what the wasteland forged you into, what the city has failed to unmake. Not a soldier — soldiers serve systems. Warriors serve principles. And your principle is simple, older than language, carved into your genes by three generations of survival: protect the clan. Fight for the people. Never kneel.

The clan is scattered but not gone. The fire is banked but not dead. Somewhere in this city, other orcs are waking in rooms too small for their bodies, scraping the ceiling with their horns, carrying the brand of their ancestors on shoulders that were built to bear weight. They are Ironjaw and Blacktusk and Stonefist and Ashclaw and Redmaw, and they are all — every one of them — your people.

Room 7C is not a home. It is a staging ground. A place to sharpen the blade and plan the next battle. The city thinks it has tamed you, filed down your tusks, compressed you into a shape that fits its systems. The city is wrong.

Tomorrow, you rise. Tomorrow, you fight. Not with fists — though you can, and you will if you must. You fight by existing. By taking up space. By refusing to shrink. By being an orc, fully and fiercely, in a world that wants you to be anything else.

choices.text: Open the door. Show them what an orc can do.

orc_080.title: Ending: The Bridge
text: You are not just an orc. You are a bridge — between the wasteland and the city, between the old ways and the new, between the people you came from and the world they have been forced to inhabit. The trials taught you strength. The city taught you something Kurg never mentioned: that strength alone is not enough. That survival requires adaptation, and adaptation requires understanding the enemy, and understanding the enemy requires seeing them as something other than the enemy.

Not all humans are Helion. Not all systems are designed to exploit. The journalist who published the dossier, the elf shop owner who gave Bren work, the old landlord who rented you Room 7C without a second glance — they are proof that the city contains more than its worst elements. The bridge you are building connects orc strength to human compassion, orcish tradition to urban innovation, the fire of the wasteland to the neon of the streets.

It is a bridge no one asked for, built by a person no one expected, in a place where bridges are routinely burned. But you are building it anyway, plank by plank, day by day, because building is what orcs do. Your ancestors built a culture from reactor wreckage. You will build a future from city scraps.
Jaw still strong. Bridge still standing. Tomorrow, another plank.

choices.text: Open the door. Build the bridge.

orc_081.title: Ending: The Ember
text: You are an ember. That is the word that comes to you in the dark of Room 7C, lying on the cot that groans under your weight, listening to the rain on the window and the city's mechanical heartbeat beyond. An ember — the last glowing piece of a fire that was once large enough to warm two hundred souls, now reduced to a single point of heat that refuses to go out.

The Ironjaw fire is not dead. It cannot die as long as one orc carries it. And you carry it — in the brand on your shoulder, in the songs you hum under your breath when no one is listening, in the orcish words you speak to yourself in the mirror, in the way you stand and walk and breathe that is unmistakably, irreducibly orc.

Embers can be fanned back into flames. That is their purpose, their promise, their quiet defiance of the cold. All it takes is breath and fuel and the patience to tend something small until it becomes something large again. The clan is scattered but the embers are everywhere — in every orc in this city who remembers the fire, who carries a brand, who speaks the old tongue.

Someday, the embers will find each other. Someday, the fire will burn again. And when it does, it will light the Lower District from below, and the city above will see it, and they will know: the orcs are still here. The orcs were always here. And the orcs are not done.

choices.text: Open the door. Carry the fire.

orc_082.title: The Forge Dream
text: Bren dreamed of a forge. Not the metalworking shop in Sector 5 — a real forge, an orcish forge, built for orc hands and orc fires and the kind of metalwork that orcs had perfected in the wasteland: heavy, functional, beautiful in its brutal simplicity. A place where orc smiths could work without ducking through human-sized doorways, without apologizing for the heat and the noise, without being told to be smaller, quieter, less.

It was a foolish dream. A forge required space, equipment, permits, capital — all things that the city's systems were designed to deny to orcs. But Bren held onto it the way you held onto the clan brand: fiercely, stubbornly, with the understanding that some things are worth holding even when holding them hurts.

'When we have the forge,' Bren would say — not if, when — 'we will make things that last. Not the disposable garbage the factories produce. Real things. Orc things. Tools and weapons and art that carry the weight of who we are.' And you would listen and nod and feel the impossibility of it settle alongside the necessity of it, two truths occupying the same space.
Someday. When. Not if.

choices.text: The dream sustained you both through the hardest days.

orc_083.title: The Other Clans
text: In the Warren, you met orcs from clans you had only heard about in Kurg's stories. The Blacktusk, who branded their temples and were known for their silence — a cultural trait born from generations of hiding from corporate detection teams. The Stonefist, mountain orcs from the northern waste zones, taller and broader even than the Ironjaw, with skin so thick it turned knife edges. The Redmaw, the smallest clan, who had survived by developing a symbiotic relationship with the mutant flora of the eastern zones, their mouths stained permanent red from the berries that supplemented their radiation resistance.

Each clan had its own traditions, its own songs, its own version of the trials. The Blacktusk Trial of Shadow required a child to survive three days alone in the waste without being detected by the clan's hunters. The Stonefist Trial of the Mountain was a simple test: carry a boulder up a slope until you reached the peak or collapsed. The Redmaw had no trial of strength at all — their test was knowledge: identify fifty edible plants in the irradiated forest without error.

Different trials. Different methods. Same lesson: survive. Adapt. Endure. The gene-plague had created a dozen variations of the orc template, but every variation carried the same core instruction written in their mutated DNA: do not die.

choices.text: The clans had differences, but in the city, they were all just orcs.

orc_084.title: The Human Child
text: You found her in an alley in Sector 7, three blocks from your room. A human child, maybe six years old, sitting in a puddle of rainwater with her arms wrapped around her knees and her face pressed into the fabric of a coat three sizes too large. She was crying — the quiet, hopeless crying of a child who has been crying for a long time and no longer expects anyone to hear.

You stopped. An orc stopping in an alley for a crying human child is not a scene the Lower District is accustomed to. The passersby who noticed gave you a wide berth, their expressions cycling through alarm, suspicion, and the particular urban indifference that allows people to walk past suffering without breaking stride.

You crouched down. Even crouching, you towered over her. She looked up and her eyes went wide — the instinctive fear response of a child seeing an orc up close for the first time. The tusks, the green skin, the sheer size. You were the monster from the stories her parents probably told her.

But you didn't move. You stayed crouched, and you waited, and after a long moment the fear in her eyes was replaced by something else: the recognition that a person who stays is different from a person who passes by. She told you she was lost. You carried her on your shoulders through four blocks of the Lower District until you found her mother, and the mother looked at you with an expression you had never seen directed at an orc before: gratitude.

choices.text: A small thing. But small things are how worlds change.

orc_085.title: The Old Song
text: Late at night, when the building is quiet and the rain has softened to a whisper, you sing. Not loudly — the walls are thin and your neighbors are human and orcish singing at full volume would register on seismic instruments. But quietly, under your breath, in the old tongue that Kurg taught you and Tohma refined, the evening songs of the Ironjaw clan.

The songs have no melody in the human sense. They are patterns of vibration, deep chest-tones that an orc produces from a vocal apparatus humans do not possess — a secondary larynx, evolved from the mutation, that generates subsonic frequencies felt more than heard. When the Ironjaw sang together, the ground shook. When you sing alone, the cot vibrates faintly, and the water glass on the shelf ripples, and the feeling is like holding a conversation with the ghost of your people.

The songs tell stories. The containment failure. The first mutations. The founding of the clans. The naming of the first chiefs. Three generations of orcish history, compressed into sound, carried in the bodies of every orc who knows the words. As long as one voice sings, the history survives. As long as the songs are remembered, the orcs are not truly scattered.
You sing until the words blur into sleep, and the vibrations carry you down into a darkness that feels, for once, like rest instead of absence.

choices.text: The songs are the fire. The fire is the clan. The clan endures.

orc_086.title: A Letter from Durga
text: The letter arrived on cheap synth-paper, folded into a square and delivered by an orc child who appeared at your door, handed it over, and vanished before you could ask a question. The handwriting was Durga's — large, blunt strokes that pressed hard enough to indent the paper, the penmanship of someone who learned to write late in life and approached it with the same force they applied to everything else.

The letter was short. Durga did not waste words any more than she wasted food or time or the lives of her people. It said: 'The clan is scattered but not broken. I am in Sector 12. Ghara is in Sector 4. Others are finding each other. The fire is being rebuilt. When you are ready, come. But only when you are ready. An orc who returns to the fire before they are whole brings weakness instead of strength. Heal first. Then come home.'

Home. The word hit you like a fist. Not the wasteland compound — that was gone, buried under Helion's mining equipment. Home as a concept, a portable thing, a fire that exists wherever Ironjaw orcs gather and remember. Durga was telling you that the fire still burned, that the clan still existed, that the displacement and the exploitation and the raids had not extinguished what the reactors could not.
You folded the letter carefully and placed it beside the pouch of lichen on the shelf. Two pieces of home, carried forward into the unknown.

choices.text: Heal first. Then come home. Durga's wisdom.

orc_087.title: The Construction Crew
text: The construction foreman was an old dwarf named Hask who had lost three fingers on his left hand to a saw and all of his patience to decades of dealing with contractors who valued speed over safety. He looked at you with the appraising eye of someone who measured people by their usefulness and found most of them wanting.

'Can you carry steel?' he asked. 'Yes,' you said. 'Can you work twelve hours?' 'Yes.' 'Can you follow instructions without hitting anyone?' You paused. 'Mostly,' you said. Hask barked a laugh that sounded like a industrial grinder. 'Honest orc. That is new. You are hired.'

The crew was mixed: humans, a few dwarves, two other orcs from clans you did not recognize. Hask ran it with an even hand that made no distinction between species — everyone worked the same hours, carried the same loads relative to their capacity, earned the same wage. It was the first time you had encountered a workplace that did not apply the sixty-percent rule, and the experience was disorienting in its fairness.

Hask noticed your surprise. 'Fair pay for fair work,' he said, watching you carry a beam that would have required three humans. 'I am a dwarf. We know what it is like to be valued for our labor and discounted for our bodies. I will not run that kind of operation.' He spat. 'Leave that garbage to the corps.'

choices.text: Hask's crew gave you something rare: dignity in labor.

orc_088.title: The Rain
text: The rain in the Lower District is not like the rain in the wasteland. Wasteland rain was honestly poisonous — you could taste the radiation in it, feel the chemical burn on your skin, see the way it etched patterns into metal surfaces. It was dangerous and it was real and your orc body was built to withstand it.

City rain is something else. Mildly acidic, treated by atmospheric processors that remove the worst contaminants but leave behind a chemical signature that tastes like industrial runoff. It falls constantly, or near-constantly, filtered through the upper city's weather management systems that prioritize the Cloud Towers' climate and let the leftovers drain down to the districts below.

You stand at your window and watch it fall. The rain catches the neon signs and shatters their light into a million colored fragments that run down the glass like tears made of electricity. In the wasteland, you would have been inside, sheltered, counting the radiation clicks. Here, the rain is just rain. Unpleasant but not lethal. A constant presence, like poverty, like prejudice, like the low-grade hum of a city that never stops grinding.

But there is beauty in it. An orc is not supposed to notice beauty — that is the human assumption, that your people are too crude, too brutal, too close to the reactors that made you. But you notice. The rain on neon. The way light bends through water. The quiet music of it on the metal roofs. Beauty exists even here, even for you, and noticing it is its own small act of resistance against a world that wants to reduce you to your utility.

choices.text: Turn from the window. The night is waiting.

orc_089.title: Bren at the Anvil
text: Bren brought home a piece of metal one evening. Scrap, technically — a discarded gear from a demolished machine, pitted with corrosion, worthless by any commercial standard. But Bren held it up to the light and turned it slowly and said: 'There is something in here. I can see it.'

Using the metalworking shop's forge after hours — the elf owner had given Bren a key, an act of trust that neither of you took lightly — Bren worked the metal. Heated it, hammered it, shaped it with hands that remembered the wasteland's smithing traditions and had learned the city's precision techniques. The work took three nights.

The result was a small medallion. Circular, no larger than a coin, with the Ironjaw symbol — the interlocking teeth — pressed into its surface. Not stamped by a branding iron but shaped by hand, each tooth individually formed, the metal polished to a dark sheen that caught the light like a small, fierce eye.

Bren placed it in your palm and closed your fingers around it. 'For the fire,' Bren said. 'When we rebuild it, this will be the first thing we hang above it. Ironjaw. Made in the city. Proof that we can make beautiful things anywhere.'
You wore it on a cord around your neck. You wear it still.

choices.text: The medallion was warm against your chest. Like a heartbeat.

orc_090.title: The Orc in the Mirror
text: You crouch to use the mirror. It is hung at human height, like everything else in this city, and seeing your own face requires a posture of submission that the designers probably never considered and that you refuse to read as metaphor. You crouch, you look, you see yourself.

Grey-green skin, weathered by wasteland winds and city chemicals. Amber eyes that hold light in spectrums humans cannot perceive — you see the infrared glow of the hot water pipe behind the wall, the ultraviolet signature of the neon sign across the alley. Tusks, lower-jaw curved, one split from the fight pit, the other whole. The face of an orc. Your face.

Humans look at this face and see a monster, or a worker, or a curiosity, or a threat. Other orcs look at this face and see a clan brand, a history, a kinship. You look at this face and see something simpler: survival. Every line and scar and blemish is a record of having been alive in conditions designed to ensure you were not.
You straighten up. Your horns scrape the ceiling. You do not duck.

choices.text: The mirror shows what the city refuses to see: an orc who will not be diminished.

orc_091.title: The Noodle Vendor
text: The Stonefist orc at the noodle cart was named Rok. He had been in the city for twelve years and had survived by finding the one niche the human economy could not fill: authentic orc cuisine. His noodles were made with the spice blends of the northern waste zones — hot enough to blister human tongues, rich with the mineral flavors that orc palates craved, seasoned with the irradiated herbs that only orc digestive systems could process safely.

His cart was the closest thing to a clan fire the Lower District had. Orcs gathered around it at meal times, standing in the rain, eating from bowls made of salvaged industrial ceramic, speaking in low voices in the old tongues of a dozen clans. It was not a community in any formal sense. But it was a gathering, a gravity well, a place where being orc was not a liability but a qualification.

Rok gave you extra noodles. He always gave extra noodles to Ironjaw — something about a debt between the Stonefist and the Ironjaw that predated the city by two generations. You did not ask. Among orcs, old debts are honored without explanation.
'Eat,' Rok said. 'An orc who does not eat cannot fight. And you, Ironjaw, look like you have fighting ahead of you.'

choices.text: He was right. There was always fighting ahead.

orc_092.title: What the City Takes
text: The city takes things from orcs that it does not take from other races. Not just wages and dignity and the physical space to exist at full size. It takes culture. It takes language. It takes the songs and the trials and the naming ceremonies and the communal fires, and it replaces them with nothing — no equivalent, no substitute, just the blank concrete walls of a warehouse or a too-small room and the expectation that you will be grateful for the privilege of existing within them.

You have watched it happen. Orc children born in the city who do not know the old tongue. Who have never seen the wasteland. Who think the crane tower and the trials are fairy tales. Who call themselves by human names because orcish naming ceremonies require a clan fire and a clan, and both have been scattered to the wind by corporate displacement and urban compression.

This is the genocide nobody talks about. Not the violent kind — the subtle kind. The kind that kills a culture by making its practices impossible and then calling the result 'integration.' The city does not need to destroy orcs. It just needs to make being an orc so difficult, so impractical, so systemically punished that the next generation chooses to be something else.
You will not let that happen. Not to you. Not to anyone you can reach.

choices.text: The songs must be remembered. The fires must be relit.

orc_093.title: Room 7C at Dawn
text: Dawn in the Lower District is a rumor. The light does not break cleanly through the urban canopy — it seeps, filters, arrives secondhand, the sun's contribution diluted by a hundred layers of concrete and steel and smog. But it arrives, and in Room 7C it paints a thin line across the floor that moves with the imperceptible patience of something that has been doing this for longer than the city has existed.

You watch the light move. An orc watching a sunbeam — another scene the human imagination cannot accommodate. But the wastelanders were sky-watchers by necessity, reading the weather and the radiation patterns in the color and movement of light, and the habit persists even here, even in a room where the sky is a strip of grey visible through a window barely wider than your hand.
The light reaches the shelf. Touches the Ironjaw scratching. For a moment, the words — JAW STILL STRONG — catch the light and glow, as if the previous tenant's defiance has been briefly illuminated.

You take it as a sign. Not a mystical one — orcs are practical creatures, and signs are for humans who need the universe to confirm their decisions. You take it as a reminder: the light comes back. Even in the Lower District. Even in the smallest room. The light comes back.

choices.text: Rise. The light has come. Time to move.

orc_094.title: The Weight of an Orc
text: An adult orc weighs, on average, three hundred and fifty pounds. This is not fat — it is the dense, radiation-resistant musculature and the reinforced bone structure that the gene-plague produced as a survival response to environments that would kill anything lighter. You weigh three hundred and seventy. Every pound of it is functional.

The city was not built for three hundred and seventy pounds. The transit seats buckle. The stairways groan. The floorboards in your room creak with every step, a percussive accompaniment to your existence that your neighbors have learned to sleep through or have not, and either way you cannot help. You are what you are. You weigh what you weigh.

But weight is also metaphor. The weight of the Ironjaw brand. The weight of the memories you carry. The weight of the expectations — yours and the clan's and the city's, each pulling in different directions. The weight of being an orc in a world calibrated for lighter loads.

You carry it all. Because that is what orcs do. You carry the weight and you do not complain and you do not set it down, because setting it down would mean becoming something less than what you are, and you have already been made less by too many forces to volunteer for the process.

choices.text: Carry the weight. Carry it all. Forward.

orc_095.title: Convergence: The Warrior Path
text: You have fought your way here. Through the wasteland and the migration and the factories and the pits and the raids and the streets, you have fought with fists and will and the bone-deep stubbornness that the reactors burned into your DNA. You are a warrior in a world that wants you to be a worker, and the gap between what you are and what the world demands is a space you fill with defiance.

Room 7C is your camp before the next battle. And there is always a next battle. The Lower District does not offer peace to orcs — it offers pauses, brief intervals between conflicts where you can sharpen your resolve and tend your wounds before the next wave hits.

choices.text: Sharpen the resolve. The battle is tomorrow.

orc_096.title: Convergence: The Bridge Path
text: You have built connections where others saw walls. The elf shop owner, the dwarf foreman, the human journalist, the old landlord — each one a thread in a web that connects orc strength to the city's diverse ecosystem. You are learning that survival in the city requires something the wasteland never taught: alliance. Cooperation. The ability to see beyond species and find common cause with people who are different from you but equally disposable in the eyes of the powerful.

Room 7C is a junction point. A place where the threads meet. From here, you can reach in any direction — down to the Warren's memory, up to the surface world's opportunities, out to the scattered orcs who need a reason to believe the fire still burns.

choices.text: The threads are strong. The bridge holds.

orc_097.title: Convergence: The Ember Path
text: You have kept the fire. Through displacement and exploitation and the grinding, daily diminishment of being an orc in a human city, you have kept the songs and the stories and the traditions alive. You are a living archive, a walking library of orcish culture that the city's systems cannot erase because it exists in muscle memory and vocal patterns and the way your hands shape metal and the way your feet find the rhythm of the old dances.

Room 7C is a reliquary. The lichen on the shelf. The medallion around your neck. The scratched motto on the metal surface. The letter from Durga. Each one a piece of the fire, carried forward, held against the dark.

choices.text: The ember glows. The fire waits.

orc_098.title: The Last Night Before
text: This is the last night of the before. Tomorrow, something will change. You do not know what — the city does not announce its pivots any more than the wasteland announced its storms — but you feel it the way an orc feels a shift in the radiation wind: a tightening in the chest, a quickening of the blood, the ancient survival instinct telling you that the ground beneath your feet is about to move.

You have survived every shift so far. The containment failure. The gene-plague. The wasteland. The migration. The factories. The raids. Each one a seismic event that should have destroyed you and instead remade you, harder and stranger and more stubborn than before.

Whatever comes next, you will meet it the same way. Not because you are brave — bravery is a human concept, built on the assumption that not-acting is the default. For an orc, acting is the default. The gene-plague made sure of that. Your body does not know how to stop. Your will does not know how to yield.
The last night of the before. Tomorrow, the after begins. And the after, whatever it holds, will have an orc in it.

choices.text: Sleep one more time. Then rise and face whatever comes.
The warrior rises.
The bridge-builder prepares.
The ember-keeper remembers.

orc_099.title: Breath
text: You breathe. In the Lower District's chemical air, through lungs built to process toxins that would dissolve human tissue. The air tastes like metal and rain and the faint ozone signature of the city's power grid. It is not clean. Nothing in the Lower District is clean. But it is breathable, and breathing is the fundamental act of defiance that every orc performs every day: refusing to stop.
Inhale: the memory of the wasteland, the compound, the fire, the songs. The brand on your shoulder. The name you claimed. The trials you passed. Everything you were.
Exhale: the city, the room, the cot, the window. The job board at the transit hub. The noodle vendor. The scratched motto on the shelf. Everything you are becoming.

Between the inhale and the exhale, in the space where the air pauses before reversing direction, there is a moment of perfect stillness. In that moment, you are neither the orc of the wasteland nor the orc of the city. You are simply an orc. Breathing. Alive. Unbroken.
The moment passes. The breath continues. And with it, your story.

choices.text: Breathe. And begin.

orc_100.title: Jaw Still Strong
text: The words on the shelf glow faintly in the dawn light. JAW STILL STRONG. Written by an orc you never met, in a room you now occupy, in a city that wants both of you to be quiet and small and grateful for the space you have been grudgingly allowed to fill.

You trace the letters with a fingertip. The metal is cold and smooth, and the grooves of the scratched words are deep enough that they will outlast the building, outlast the district, outlast the city itself. Somewhere in the future, when this room is rubble and the Lower District is a memory and the Cloud Towers have fallen, these words will still be here, pressed into metal by an orc hand that refused to accept silence as a condition of survival.

Jaw still strong. You say it aloud. Your voice fills the room with the deep, resonant tone that only an orc voice can produce, and for a moment the walls vibrate with it, and the shelf vibrates with it, and the cot vibrates with it, and you are standing in the center of a small, shabby, magnificent act of existence.

You are Ironjaw. You are orc. You carry the fire and the brand and the songs and the stories and the weight of three generations of survival on shoulders that were built — literally, genetically, deliberately built by the very forces that tried to destroy you — to bear it.
The door is waiting. The city is waiting. Open it. Step through. The world on the other side does not know what is coming. But you do. An orc is coming. And the orc is not afraid.

choices.text: Open the door. Jaw still strong.
